steps.instructions: Check for wet or clogged bacterial/HEPA filters in the inspiratory and expiratory limbs. Inspect humidifier chamber for condensation blocking flow sensors.

steps.title: Assess Leak Alarms and Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm that leak compensation is correctly set according to patient interface. Check that flow sensors are clean, dry, and seated properly.

steps.title: Battery or Power Check
steps.instructions: Verify ventilator is on reliable mains power or battery with adequate charge. Low battery or unstable power can cause inconsistent flow delivery.

steps.title: Perform a Quick Self-Test (if safe to do)
steps.instructions: Use the Trilogy’s built-in self-test to verify sensor functionality and system integrity. Document any error codes for further analysis.

CCR.Complaint: Patient tidal volume readings are inconsistent; flow delivery seems erratic.
CCR.Cause: Loose circuit connection identified; mask fit suboptimal; filters partially obstructed.
CCR.Resolution: Re-secured circuit, adjusted mask interface, replaced filters, verified self-test passed, monitored tidal volume for stability.

title: Philips Trilogy Ventilator - Humidifier or Accessory Connection Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting humidifier or accessory connection errors on a Philips Trilogy ventilator, focusing on external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Verify Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient is stable and, if possible, transfer them to another functioning ventilator before troubleshooting. Never attempt to resolve connection issues while the device is actively supporting a patient.

steps.title: Inspect the Humidifier Unit
steps.instructions: Confirm the humidifier is seated properly in its docking area. Misalignment can trigger errors. Check that the water chamber is correctly inserted and the lid is secure. Ensure the humidifier’s power connector (if separate) is fully engaged.

steps.title: Check the Ventilator Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm that all tubing, Y-pieces, and adapters are securely attached to the ventilator ports. Examine connectors for visible damage, debris, or condensation that could interfere with proper detection. Swap tubing with a known-good circuit to rule out faulty accessories.

steps.title: Inspect Electrical and Data Interfaces
steps.instructions: If the humidifier has an electrical interface, check the cable for damage or loose connections. Confirm that the ventilator’s accessory ports are free from dust or corrosion.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Turn the ventilator off, wait 10–15 seconds, then power back on. Observe whether the device now recognizes the humidifier or accessory.

steps.title: Check Device Settings
steps.instructions: Access the ventilator menu to confirm that the humidifier function is enabled. Verify that any accessory-specific settings match the connected device.

steps.title: Observe Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the previous alarm has cleared. Document any persistent alarm codes for reference in repair or service escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarm shows ‘humidifier not detected’ despite proper installation.
CCR.Cause: Observed that the humidifier was correctly installed, all tubing connections intact, and cables undamaged; ventilator failed to recognize accessory.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for Philips-authorized repair.

title: Philips Avalon FM20 Fetal Monitor – Fetal Heart Rate Signal Loss or Poor Tracing
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot Avalon FM20 fetal monitors reporting FHR signal loss or poor-quality tracings.

steps.title: Verify Power and System Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is powered on and the battery is charged or plugged into AC. Check that all system self-tests complete successfully. Why: Power instability or incomplete system startup can cause signal dropouts.

steps.title: Inspect Transducer Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all transducer cables are firmly connected and not damaged. Swap the transducer cable with a known good one if available. Why: Loose or damaged cables are a common cause of poor tracing.

steps.title: Check Transducer Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the FHR transducer is correctly positioned on the maternal abdomen. Reposition and secure using proper gel and belt tension. Why: Misplacement reduces signal quality and may mimic signal loss.

steps.title: Verify Maternal Factors and Environment
steps.instructions: Check for excessive maternal movement or interference from other electronic devices. Minimize patient motion if possible and move away from sources of electrical noise. Why: Motion and interference can distort or interrupt the fetal signal.

steps.title: Test with Alternate Transducer
steps.instructions: Use a spare, compatible FHR transducer to determine if the original transducer is faulty. Why: Helps isolate the issue to a single transducer versus system error.

steps.title: Check Monitor Settings
steps.instructions: Verify the sensitivity, alarm limits, and channel selection. Ensure the correct patient configuration is selected (singleton vs. multiple gestation). Why: Incorrect settings can reduce signal detection or create false alarms.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported intermittent FHR signal loss and poor-quality tracings on Avalon FM20 monitor.
CCR.Cause: Observed loose transducer connection and poor abdominal placement. Swapped cable and repositioned transducer; patient movement may also contribute.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected transducer and repositioned properly. Swapped cable to confirm integrity. Monitor functioning correctly. If issue persists, monitor will be sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Monitoring resumed after repositioning

title: Philips Avalon FM20 Fetal Monitor – Maternal Heart Rate Cross-Talk or Misreading
description: Guide for troubleshooting Avalon FM20 fetal monitors displaying maternal heart rate as fetal heart rate or showing cross-talk between signals.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Check that the maternal ECG or TOCO and fetal sensors are correctly positioned according to the manufacturer’s guidelines. Misplaced sensors can cause the monitor to confuse maternal and fetal signals.

steps.title: Inspect Sensor Condition
steps.instructions: Examine abdominal and maternal electrodes for wear, damage, or dried gel. Replace any faulty or dried electrodes; poor contact can lead to inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Check Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all leads are fully seated in the monitor ports. Loose or corroded connections can introduce signal interference.

steps.title: Swap Electrodes or Leads if Needed
steps.instructions: If the monitor continues to show cross-talk, swap the maternal or fetal leads with known-good ones. This helps identify if the problem is with the cable or connector rather than the monitor.

steps.title: Reduce Signal Interference
steps.instructions: Remove or distance other electrical equipment nearby (infusion pumps, phones, monitors). Electrical interference can cause the monitor to misinterpret signals.

steps.title: Verify Monitor Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor’s maternal/fetal detection settings are correct. Reset to default configuration if uncertain; misconfigured settings may contribute to misreadings.

steps.title: Perform a Self-Test or Signal Check
steps.instructions: Use the monitor’s internal test functions if available to verify sensor detection and signal processing.

CCR.Complaint: Fetal monitor shows maternal heart rate as fetal heart rate and exhibits cross-talk.
CCR.Cause: Maternal and fetal electrodes were incorrectly placed and one lead showed poor contact. No internal hardware fault detected.
CCR.Resolution: Electrodes repositioned per manufacturer guidelines. Leads replaced where necessary. Monitor verified with test strip; cross-talk resolved.

helpfulDetails: Check for electrode integrity and gel condition.
Note any alarm messages or unusual monitor behavior.
Document final monitor status after troubleshooting.

title: Philips Avalon FM20 Fetal Monitor – Uterine Contraction Sensor Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting TOCO sensor errors on the Philips Avalon FM20 fetal monitor, ensuring accurate maternal contraction monitoring.

steps.title: Check Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the TOCO sensor cable is securely connected to the monitor. Loose or intermittent connections are a common cause of signal errors.

steps.title: Inspect Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the TOCO sensor is positioned correctly on the patient’s abdomen, over the uterine fundus. Incorrect placement can cause weak or erratic readings.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Strap Tension
steps.instructions: The elastic strap should be snug but not overly tight. Too loose → poor signal; too tight → patient discomfort and inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Check for Interference or Motion
steps.instructions: Patient movement, coughing, or talking can temporarily disrupt the TOCO signal. Ask the patient to remain still for a moment and observe signal stabilization.

steps.title: Swap Cables or Sensor (if available)
steps.instructions: Use a known-good TOCO sensor and/or cable to rule out a faulty component.

steps.title: Inspect Monitor Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is set to detect TOCO signals and that alarm thresholds are appropriate.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is on a stable power source or fully charged battery. Voltage fluctuations can sometimes affect sensor readings.

CCR.Complaint: TOCO sensor error alarm reported; no reliable uterine contraction tracing.
CCR.Cause: Sensor cable loosely connected; patient movement detected; TOCO sensor misaligned.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned TOCO sensor over uterine fundus, adjusted strap tension, and replaced cable with a known-good spare. Signal verified; tracing stable.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: working OR removed from service

title: Philips Avalon FM20 Fetal Monitor – Paper Recorder or Printout Failure
description: Guide to troubleshoot Avalon FM20 monitor paper recorder failures, including blank or incomplete printouts.

steps.title: Check Paper Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct type and size of paper is loaded. Ensure paper is not jammed or folded. Why: The recorder cannot print without properly loaded paper.

steps.title: Inspect Printer Door and Paper Path
steps.instructions: Verify the printer door is fully closed and the paper path is clear of obstructions. Remove any debris or small torn pieces of paper. Why: Mechanical sensors may prevent printing if the door isn’t closed or path is blocked.

steps.title: Verify Recorder Settings
steps.instructions: Check the monitor’s menu settings for print mode and interval. Ensure the recorder is enabled and the correct channel(s) are selected. Why: Incorrect configuration can prevent prints even if the paper is loaded.

steps.title: Inspect Printer Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure cables (if external) between monitor and printer are secure. If the printer is built-in, confirm internal connectors have not loosened. Why: Loose connections can prevent data from reaching the recorder.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Paper Roll
steps.instructions: Swap the paper with a new roll to rule out defects or moisture damage. Why: Faulty paper can cause jams or blank printouts.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off, wait 30 seconds, then power back on. Attempt a test print. Why: A software glitch can sometimes prevent printing; restarting can reset the printer subsystem.

CCR.Complaint: User reports that the Avalon FM20 monitor fails to print paper records.
CCR.Cause: Paper not loaded correctly, printer door not closed, or communication issue with recorder.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared jams, verified paper, confirmed settings, and tested print function. Escalated to repair if issue persists.

title: Philips Avalon FM20 Fetal Monitor – Connectivity / Data Export Issues
description: Guide for troubleshooting connectivity and data export problems on the Avalon FM20 fetal monitor.

steps.title: Check Power and Device Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is powered on and functioning normally. Verify battery level or AC power connection; low power can disrupt communication. Why: Ensures the device is operational before network troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Network Connections
steps.instructions: Check Ethernet or Wi-Fi connectivity. Ensure cables are fully seated and undamaged. Confirm the monitor’s network settings (IP address, subnet, gateway) match your network requirements. Why: Poor or misconfigured network connections are a common cause of failed data export.

steps.title: Test Data Export Function
steps.instructions: Attempt manual export to EMR or central monitoring station. Observe for error messages (e.g., 'Connection Failed' or 'Data Transfer Error'). Why: Confirms if the issue is consistent or intermittent.

steps.title: Check Central System / EMR Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the receiving system is online and accepting data from the Avalon FM20. Check firewall or security settings that may block device communication. Why: Connectivity issues may originate outside the monitor itself.

steps.title: Swap Cables or Ports if Wired
steps.instructions: Replace the Ethernet cable or try a different network port. Why: Eliminates physical cable/port failure as the cause.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled reboot to refresh the network stack and device processes. Why: Temporary software glitches can be resolved by restarting.

steps.title: Review Device Logs
steps.instructions: Check error logs or event history for communication failures. Why: Provides insight into the nature of export failures.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor fails to send patient data to EMR; error message displays ‘Connection Failed.’
CCR.Cause: Network settings and connectivity verified; cables and central system operational; monitor unable to communicate with server.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair to Biomedical Engineering bench.

title: Philips Avalon FM30 Fetal Monitor – Fetal / Maternal Heart Rate Signal Interference or Dropout
description: Troubleshooting fetal and maternal heart rate signal interference, cross-talk, or dropout on the Philips Avalon FM30 fetal monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being monitored appropriately on another device if needed. Do not troubleshoot while actively relied upon for critical monitoring.

steps.title: Check Transducer Placement and Contact
steps.instructions: Ensure ultrasound (US) transducer is properly positioned over the fetal heart. Verify adequate gel is used. Reposition slightly to improve signal acquisition. Poor placement is the most common cause of signal dropout.

steps.title: Differentiate Fetal vs Maternal Signal
steps.instructions: Compare displayed heart rate with maternal pulse. If values are similar, the monitor may be detecting maternal heart rate. This is a known limitation—maternal vessels can be picked up by the transducer.

steps.title: Check All Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect transducer cables for damage, kinks, or loose connections. Reseat connectors firmly into the monitor. Swap with a known-good transducer if available.

steps.title: Inspect Belts and Securing Method
steps.instructions: Ensure belts are snug enough to maintain consistent contact. Loose belts can cause intermittent dropout during patient movement.

steps.title: Evaluate for Electromagnetic / RF Interference
steps.instructions: Look for nearby sources: wireless devices (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cordless phones) and other medical equipment. Move the monitor or transducers away from interference sources. These devices are sensitive to RF interference, which can degrade signal quality.

steps.title: Check Cableless / Telemetry (If Used)
steps.instructions: Verify base station connection and antenna integrity. Ensure patient is within range. Interference or range issues can cause intermittent signal loss.

steps.title: Review Alarm Messages and Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for INOP messages, signal quality indicators, and dropout alarms. Document any recurring alarm patterns.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Replace ultrasound transducer and TOCO sensor (if applicable). Helps isolate accessory vs monitor issue.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Turn off, wait 10–15 seconds, and restart. Clears temporary processing or communication issues.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported intermittent fetal heart rate dropout and occasional matching maternal heart rate values.
CCR.Cause: Loose ultrasound transducer placement and interference from nearby wireless equipment causing inconsistent signal acquisition.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned transducer, replaced worn belt, and relocated monitor away from interference source. Verified stable FHR tracing.

helpfulDetails: Wireless interference identified or ruled out
Maternal vs fetal HR comparison performed
Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Philips Avalon FM30 Fetal Monitor – Uterine Contraction Sensor Misalignment or Error
description: Troubleshooting uterine contraction (TOCO) sensor misalignment, inaccurate readings, or detection errors on the Philips Avalon FM30 fetal monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is not actively being relied on for patient care before troubleshooting. Move patient monitoring to another device if needed.

steps.title: Inspect TOCO Transducer Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the TOCO sensor is positioned correctly on the uterine fundus. Misalignment is the most common cause of inaccurate contraction readings. Reposition and secure with the belt to ensure firm but comfortable contact.

steps.title: Check Belt and Sensor Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the belt is not too loose or overly tight. Loose sensors can cause drifting or absent readings. Tight belts may distort readings or cause patient discomfort.

steps.title: Inspect Cable and Connector
steps.instructions: Check the TOCO cable for visible damage, kinks, or fraying. Ensure the connector is fully seated in the correct port on the monitor. Partially connected cables can cause intermittent errors.

steps.title: Swap with Known Good TOCO Transducer
steps.instructions: Replace the sensor with a known working unit. This helps quickly determine if the issue is accessory-related.

steps.title: Check Monitor Recognition
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor detects the TOCO transducer when connected. Look for error messages such as “Transducer Not Detected” or abnormal baseline readings.

steps.title: Zero / Reset TOCO Sensor (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure the TOCO baseline is properly zeroed per clinical workflow. An incorrect baseline can mimic misalignment or inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Inspect for Environmental or Patient Factors
steps.instructions: Excessive patient movement, repositioning, or obesity can affect readings. Ensure consistent placement and minimize movement during testing.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor
steps.instructions: Power cycle the unit to clear temporary software or detection issues.

steps.title: Test with Simulator (if available)
steps.instructions: Use a fetal/maternal simulator to verify proper TOCO response. Confirms whether the issue is patient/setup-related versus device-related.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported uterine contractions not displaying or inconsistent on Avalon FM30.
CCR.Cause: TOCO transducer found loosely positioned and intermittently losing contact; belt not secured properly.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned TOCO sensor on uterine fundus, secured belt, and verified stable readings. Tested with known good transducer to confirm proper function.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (in service or sent for repair)

title: Philips Avalon FM30 Fetal Monitor – Alarm or Alert Misconfiguration
description: Troubleshooting alarm or alert issues such as muted alarms, incorrect limits, or unexpected alert behavior on the Avalon FM30.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Always confirm the patient is on an alternate monitoring device before making any adjustments. Do not troubleshoot alarm functionality on an active patient.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm what the user is experiencing (no alarms, constant alarms, incorrect limits, etc.). Observe the monitor in operation if possible. This helps determine if the issue is configuration-related versus hardware failure.

steps.title: Check Alarm Volume and Silence Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the alarm volume is turned up and not muted. Look for any “Audio Off” or “Alarm Silence” indicators on the display. Temporary silencing may be mistaken for alarm failure.

steps.title: Review Alarm Limits
steps.instructions: Navigate to alarm settings and verify fetal heart rate (FHR) and maternal heart rate (MHR) limits. Compare against hospital policy or standard defaults. Incorrect limits can prevent alarms from triggering appropriately.

steps.title: Check Alarm Mode / Profile Settings
steps.instructions: Verify if the monitor is in a specific mode (e.g., demo mode, standby, or custom profile). Ensure the correct patient type/profile is selected. Profiles may carry different preset alarm configurations.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Activation
steps.instructions: Ensure alarms are enabled for all monitored parameters (FHR, MHR, TOCO). Some parameters may have alarms individually disabled.

steps.title: Inspect for Central Monitoring Overrides
steps.instructions: If connected to a central station, verify whether alarm settings are being controlled remotely. Central systems may override local alarm behavior.

steps.title: Cycle Power to Reset Temporary Settings
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off, wait 10–15 seconds, and power it back on. This can clear temporary configuration glitches.

steps.title: Check for Recent Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Ask staff if alarm settings were recently adjusted. Reset to default settings if misconfiguration is suspected and appropriate.

steps.title: Test Alarm Functionality
steps.instructions: Simulate a condition (if safe and appropriate) to verify alarms trigger correctly. Confirm both visual and audible alarms function as expected.

steps.title: Inspect User Interface Responsiveness
steps.instructions: Ensure buttons and touchscreen inputs respond correctly when adjusting alarm settings. Unresponsive controls may indicate a deeper issue.

CCR.Complaint: “Alarms not sounding for abnormal fetal heart rate” or “Alarm limits appear incorrect and not alerting staff.”
CCR.Cause: Alarm volume found muted and FHR limits set outside normal clinical range.
CCR.Resolution: Adjusted alarm volume, restored default alarm limits per policy, and verified proper alarm function through testing.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Philips Avalon FM30 Fetal Monitor – Paper Recorder or Printout Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting paper recorder issues on the Avalon FM30 fetal monitor, including no printout, faint/incomplete traces, paper feed problems, or misaligned printing.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor Has Power and Is Fully Booted
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is powered on and not in standby. Printing will not occur if the monitor is not actively monitoring or recording.

steps.title: Confirm Recorder Is Enabled
steps.instructions: Check that the recorder function is turned on and that recording has been initiated (manual or automatic). Some units require pressing a “Start/Stop Recording” button.

steps.title: Check Paper Supply
steps.instructions: Open the recorder door and verify paper is present, not empty or near end, and properly seated in the tray.

steps.title: Verify Correct Paper Type and Orientation
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct thermal paper is being used and loaded in the proper direction. Paper should feed from the bottom and print side should face the print head. Incorrect orientation will result in blank printouts.

steps.title: Inspect Paper Path for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Look for torn paper fragments, debris, or misaligned paper rolls. Clear any obstructions and reload paper cleanly.

steps.title: Check Recorder Door Closure
steps.instructions: Ensure the recorder door is fully closed and latched. A partially open door will prevent printing.

steps.title: Assess Print Quality (Faint or Missing Lines)
steps.instructions: If printing is faint or incomplete, try a new roll of paper, ensure paper is not expired or heat-damaged, and check for consistent pressure along the paper path.

steps.title: Perform a Test Print
steps.instructions: Initiate a test strip or recording. Observe if paper feeds correctly and check for consistent tracing output. This helps determine if the issue is continuous or intermittent.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or Indicators
steps.instructions: Review the display for recorder-related alerts such as 'Out of paper', 'Recorder error', or 'Door open'. Address any indicated issues accordingly.

steps.title: Inspect for Mechanical Issues
steps.instructions: Listen for abnormal sounds during printing. Grinding or slipping may indicate a worn feed mechanism. No movement may indicate motor or drive failure.

CCR.Complaint: “Monitor not printing fetal strip” or “Printout is blank/faint during monitoring.”
CCR.Cause: Paper loaded incorrectly (wrong orientation) preventing thermal printing.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded paper correctly and verified proper printout with test strip.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (operational or removed from service)

title: Philips Avalon FM30 Fetal Monitor – Software or Network Interface Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting software or network interface failures on the Avalon FM30 fetal monitor, focusing on external checks before escalation.

steps.title: Verify Power and Boot Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is fully powered on. Check that all status indicators (power, network link lights) are illuminated appropriately. Devices can fail to interface if not fully powered or stuck during boot.

steps.title: Check Network Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect Ethernet or Wi-Fi connections for secure plugs, intact cables, and active network ports. Swap cables if needed. Verify connection to the correct VLAN or network segment. Loose or misconfigured network connections are the most common cause of interface failures.

steps.title: Confirm Network Availability
steps.instructions: Ping the monitor from a network workstation, if available, to confirm reachability. Check that the DHCP server (if used) is assigning a valid IP. The monitor requires an active network to communicate with EMR or central monitoring.

steps.title: Review Software Status
steps.instructions: Check for any system alerts or software error messages. Perform a soft reboot of the monitor using the built-in restart function. Temporary software faults can often be cleared with a controlled restart.

steps.title: Verify EMR / Central Monitoring Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure correct IP addresses, hostnames, and port numbers are entered in the monitor configuration. Confirm that network credentials, if required, are correct. Misconfigured software settings prevent successful communication even if the network is operational.

steps.title: Check for Recent Updates or Patches
steps.instructions: Confirm that the monitor is running the latest approved software version. Note any recent software changes that coincide with the start of failures. Software bugs or incomplete updates can trigger network interface errors.

steps.title: Document Any Error Codes
steps.instructions: Record specific error codes displayed on the monitor or network logs. Provides information for escalation and repair if needed.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor will not connect to central monitoring; network interface error displayed.
CCR.Cause: Verified power, network cable integrity, and software configuration; error persists after reboot.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to Biomedical Engineering bench for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: IP address and network ping results

title: Philips HeartStart FRx - Battery Not Recognized or Status Indicator Alerts
description: Addresses unrecognized battery or status light alerts, focusing on battery seating, contact integrity, and external power verification.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the defibrillator is not in active use. If a patient requires resuscitation, provide a backup AED immediately.

steps.title: Check Battery Seating
steps.instructions: Remove the battery and reinstall firmly. Proper seating ensures electrical contact for device operation. Expected Outcome: Status light clears; device powers on normally.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Check both the battery terminals and the device contacts for dirt, corrosion, or debris. Clean gently if needed. Expected Outcome: Good contact restores battery recognition.

steps.title: Verify Battery Type and Expiration
steps.instructions: Confirm the battery is compatible (typically CR123A lithium for FRx) and not expired. Replace if incorrect or past expiration. Expected Outcome: Device recognizes battery correctly.

steps.title: Check Status Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Observe the device’s status indicator after battery reseating or replacement. Ensure the “OK” light illuminates. Expected Outcome: Green “OK” light indicates readiness.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Battery
steps.instructions: Use a tested spare battery to rule out battery failure. Expected Outcome: Device recognizes the spare battery; confirms original battery fault if issue resolves.

steps.title: Firmware or Device Reset
steps.instructions: If battery and contacts are confirmed good, power-cycle the AED or perform a device reset per standard FRx procedure. Expected Outcome: Device reinitializes and recognizes the battery.

CCR.Complaint: AED shows battery not recognized and status light alert during routine check.
CCR.Cause: Original battery failed or device contacts were dirty.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned contacts and reseated battery; replaced with new battery when necessary; verified green “OK” light.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or charging status verified if rechargeable battery
Status light behavior before and after troubleshooting
Any unusual sounds or heat during testing
Final device status: ready for patient use

title: Philips HeartStart FRx - Pad Cartridge Not Detected or Improperly Connected
description: Addresses pad cartridge detection issues, focusing on connection integrity, cartridge seating, and external inspection before internal service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the defibrillator is not in active use. If a patient requires resuscitation, provide a backup AED immediately.

steps.title: Inspect Pad Cartridge
steps.instructions: Remove the pad cartridge. Check for visible damage, expiration date, and ensure the adhesive pads are intact. Damaged or expired cartridges may fail detection.

steps.title: Verify Cartridge Orientation
steps.instructions: Reinsert the pad cartridge according to the device’s orientation guides. Proper seating ensures electrical contact with the defibrillator.

steps.title: Check Connector Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect the cartridge connector for dirt, debris, or corrosion. Clean contacts gently with a lint-free cloth if needed. Poor contact can prevent pad detection.

steps.title: Listen for Device Confirmation
steps.instructions: When the cartridge is correctly inserted, the HeartStart FRx typically emits a brief beep and updates the status indicator. Confirm the “Ready” light or icon is illuminated.

steps.title: Try a Known Good Cartridge
steps.instructions: If available, insert a different, verified working pad cartridge. If the AED detects this cartridge, the original pad cartridge is likely faulty.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions: Turn the AED off and back on after reinserting the cartridge. Some detection errors clear after a power cycle.

CCR.Complaint: AED displays “Pad Not Detected” upon power-on.
CCR.Cause: Pad cartridge connector contacts dirty and/or cartridge improperly seated.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned contacts, reseated cartridge, and verified device Ready indicator illuminated.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual odors or damage to pads

title: Philips HeartStart FRx - Self-Test Failure on Power-On
description: Addresses failures during the HeartStart FRx self-test on power-on, focusing on battery integrity, pad cartridge status, and external device readiness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the defibrillator is not in active use. If a patient requires resuscitation, provide a backup device.

steps.title: Check Battery Status
steps.instructions:
Verify the battery is fully seated and the contacts are clean.
Expect the device to power on normally and begin self-test.
Loose or depleted batteries often cause self-test failures.

steps.title: Inspect Pad Cartridge
steps.instructions:
Confirm the pad cartridge is installed correctly and connectors are clean.
Self-test checks pad connectivity; improperly seated pads can trigger failure.

steps.title: Examine Device Exterior
steps.instructions:
Look for dirt, moisture, or damage to connectors and ports.
Clean as needed; debris may interrupt self-test detection.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions:
Remove battery and pad cartridge, wait 30 seconds, reinsert, and power on.
Observe if self-test completes successfully. This resets minor firmware or connection errors.

steps.title: Check Indicator Lights and Alerts
steps.instructions:
Ensure status indicator lights show normal (green).
Record any error codes or audible prompts for documentation.

steps.title: Swap Battery if Available
steps.instructions:
Use a known good battery to isolate the battery as a cause.
If self-test passes with replacement, original battery is likely faulty.

CCR.Complaint: Device fails self-test upon power-on, alerting clinical staff.
CCR.Cause: Battery seating and pad cartridge verified; issue persists. Likely internal fault.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Pad cartridge type and installation verification
Any error codes or audible alerts

title: Philips HeartStart FRx - Voice Prompt or Audio Guidance Not Activating
description: Addresses failure of the FRx to provide voice prompts or audio guidance, focusing on battery, pad cartridge, speaker function, and external settings.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the defibrillator is not in active use. If a patient requires resuscitation, use an alternate AED immediately.

steps.title: Check Battery Connection and Status
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the battery. Verify battery is fully charged and recognized (status light green). Poor battery contact can prevent audio circuits from powering.

steps.title: Inspect Pad Cartridge
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the electrode pads cartridge. Ensure cartridge is the correct model and fully connected. Pads not detected can inhibit voice prompts during self-test.

steps.title: Perform Device Self-Test
steps.instructions: Power the AED on without pads attached (if allowed). Listen for any initial audio prompt. Note whether the self-test passes or fails; a failure may indicate internal or speaker fault.

steps.title: Check Volume Settings
steps.instructions: Locate the volume control on the FRx (if available). Ensure it is not muted or set too low.

steps.title: Inspect for Obstructions or Damage
steps.instructions: Examine speaker grill for debris, dust, or moisture. Check for cracks or physical damage that could prevent sound.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Battery and Pads
steps.instructions: Swap in a confirmed functional battery and pad cartridge. Power on and listen for voice prompts. If audio activates, original battery or pad cartridge may be faulty.

CCR.Complaint: AED voice prompts not activating during self-test.
CCR.Cause: Battery and/or pad cartridge not providing proper contact or internal speaker fault suspected.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service; battery and pads tested; unit labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Pad cartridge tested and recognized: Yes/No
Speaker integrity observed: Any unusual sounds or obstruction noted
Device status after troubleshooting: Out of Service

title: Philips HeartStart FRx - Shock Delivery Failure or Defibrillation Error
description: Addresses failure to deliver a defibrillation shock, focusing on external connections, battery, pads, and device readiness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the device is not in active use. If a patient requires resuscitation, use an alternate defibrillator immediately. Patient safety is the priority before any troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify the battery is correctly seated and fully charged. Look for low battery or status indicator alerts. A weak or unrecognized battery can prevent shock delivery.

steps.title: Inspect Pad Cartridge
steps.instructions: Ensure the pad cartridge is correctly inserted and fully seated. Check that the connectors are clean, dry, and undamaged. Pads not properly connected will interrupt shock circuit completion.

steps.title: Examine Pad Placement and Leads
steps.instructions: Inspect cables and connectors for damage or wear. Ensure electrode pads themselves are within shelf life and not dried out. Damaged or expired pads can prevent effective defibrillation.

steps.title: Confirm Device Readiness and Alerts
steps.instructions: Check for any error messages, status lights, or chirps indicating a device fault. Review the device self-test status if available. The HeartStart FRx will inhibit shocks if a critical error is detected.

steps.title: Attempt a Device Test Shock (if safe and appropriate)
steps.instructions: Use the built-in test feature if available and no patient is attached. Confirm whether the device can charge and discharge without a load. If test shock succeeds, issue may be patient/pad interface or clinical setup.

CCR.Complaint: Device failed to deliver shock during a clinical simulation.
CCR.Cause: Pad cartridge improperly seated and battery slightly depleted.
CCR.Resolution: Re-seated pad cartridge, confirmed battery full, device test successful.

helpfulDetails: Pad cartridge inspection and expiration date

title: Philips Affiniti Series Ultrasound System - Probe Not Recognized or Connection Error
description: Addresses probe detection failures caused by connection issues, probe faults, port errors, or software recognition issues before assuming internal hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Remove the probe from the patient. If imaging is needed urgently, transition the patient to another functioning system. This prevents patient care interruption during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Probe and Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Check that the probe connector is fully seated in the system port. Look for bent pins, debris, or visible damage. Re-seat the probe and attempt detection again. A loose or dirty connection is a common cause of recognition errors.

steps.title: Swap Probe or Port
steps.instructions: Connect a known working probe to the same port. Connect the suspect probe to another port if available. If the problem follows the probe, the probe may be faulty. If the problem follows the port, the system connection may be the issue.

steps.title: Check System Settings and Software
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct probe type is selected in the system menu. Restart the ultrasound system to clear temporary software locks. Software recognition errors can prevent the probe from being detected.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Interference Issues
steps.instructions: Ensure no strong electromagnetic interference nearby. Confirm the system has been powered down and stabilized if recently moved. Environmental factors can cause intermittent connection errors.

steps.title: Observe Indicators and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Note any error codes, warning lights, or sounds from the system. Document these for work order and potential escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Probe not recognized when connected to the ultrasound system.
CCR.Cause: Connector pins clean but system failed to detect probe; suspected internal or probe fault.
CCR.Resolution: System labeled Out of Service and sent for Philips service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any error codes or unusual sounds noted
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Philips Affiniti Series Ultrasound System - Image Artifact or Quality Degradation
description: Addresses poor image quality or artifacts caused by probe issues, settings, connections, or environmental factors before assuming internal system failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Remove the probe from the patient. If imaging is urgent, move the patient to another functional system.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe and Connector
steps.instructions: Check the probe for visible damage, cracks, or debris. Inspect the connector for bent pins or debris. Expected outcome: Probe and connector are physically intact.

steps.title: Verify Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the probe cable is fully seated in the system port. Try another compatible port if available. Expected outcome: Secure connections; artifacts persist only if the issue is internal or probe-specific.

steps.title: Swap Probes
steps.instructions: If multiple probes are available, connect a different probe of the same type. Expected outcome: If image improves, original probe is likely at fault.

steps.title: Check Imaging Settings
steps.instructions: Reset presets or standard imaging modes. Adjust gain, depth, and focus to default values. Expected outcome: Image artifacts resolve if caused by incorrect settings.

steps.title: Environmental and Interference Check
steps.instructions: Ensure no strong electromagnetic sources are nearby (e.g., mobile devices, cautery). Verify monitor and system are on stable power. Expected outcome: Artifacts reduce or disappear if interference was the cause.

steps.title: Perform System Self-Test / QC
steps.instructions: Run built-in probe or image quality checks. Expected outcome: Identify probe-specific faults flagged by system diagnostics.

CCR.Complaint: Images show streaking and unclear tissue borders on the Affiniti system.
CCR.Cause: Probe connector had slight contamination; settings partially misadjusted.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned probe connector, reset imaging presets; image quality verified normal.

helpfulDetails: Cables swapped with known working probe
Artifacts type and pattern (e.g., streaks, dropouts)
No unusual heat or smell detected

title: Philips Affiniti Series Ultrasound System - System Slow, Freezing, or Crashing
description: Addresses slow performance, system freezing, or unexpected crashes caused by software issues, memory overload, connections, or environmental factors before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not being used for active imaging. Move any patient requiring imaging to another functional system.

steps.title: Check Power Stability
steps.instructions: Verify the system is plugged into a dedicated, grounded outlet. Inspect power cords for damage. Unstable power can cause freezing or crashes.

steps.title: Close Unnecessary Applications / Reboot
steps.instructions: If safe, perform a controlled system reboot. Ensure no background tasks are overloading memory. Observe if performance improves after reboot.

steps.title: Inspect Connections and Peripherals
steps.instructions: Disconnect any non-essential USB devices or network drives. Loose or faulty peripherals can cause software instability.

steps.title: Check System Storage and Memory Usage
steps.instructions: Verify sufficient free disk space. If storage is nearly full, clear old studies or temporary files. Insufficient memory can lead to slowness or crashes.

steps.title: Verify Network Connections
steps.instructions: Slow or unstable network connections can cause freezing during DICOM transfers or database access. Test network cable and switch port.

steps.title: Check for Software Updates or Errors
steps.instructions: Review system logs for repeated error messages. Confirm the system firmware and software version are current per hospital IT or vendor guidance. Apply updates if available and approved by IT.

steps.title: Observe for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is not overheating. Confirm adequate ventilation and that air vents are unobstructed.

steps.title: Test with Minimal Configuration
steps.instructions: Remove non-critical probes or accessories. Boot the system with only essential devices connected to isolate potential conflicts.

CCR.Complaint: System intermittently freezes and crashes during routine imaging.
CCR.Cause: System memory near full and multiple background processes causing slow performance.
CCR.Resolution: Rebooted system, removed unnecessary applications, cleared temporary files, and monitored performance.

title: Philips Affiniti Series Ultrasound System - Touchscreen or Trackball Failure
description: Addresses unresponsive touchscreen or trackball caused by calibration drift, connection issues, environmental interference, or software faults before assuming internal hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not actively imaging a patient. Move any patient to another functional ultrasound system if imaging is urgent.

steps.title: Verify External Connections and Power
steps.instructions: Check that the system is connected to a stable AC outlet. Confirm the system is fully powered on and no low-battery warnings exist (for battery backup models). Observe indicator lights on the control panel for errors.

steps.title: Inspect Touchscreen and Trackball Hardware
steps.instructions: Clean touchscreen and trackball surfaces with approved cleaning solutions; debris can prevent proper input. Check for physical damage or loosened connections at the trackball port or touchscreen connector.

steps.title: Test Input Devices
steps.instructions: Attempt basic menu navigation using touchscreen. If touchscreen is unresponsive, try the trackball (and vice versa) to determine if one device functions. Swap trackball with a known-good unit if available to rule out device-specific failure.

steps.title: Check System Settings
steps.instructions: Navigate to system calibration settings if possible. Run touchscreen or trackball recalibration routines according to on-screen prompts. Observe if recalibration improves responsiveness.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Perform a full system shutdown and power cycle. Power back on and test touchscreen and trackball again. This can clear temporary software lockups affecting input devices.

steps.title: Observe Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure there is no strong electromagnetic interference (nearby monitors, phones, or heaters). Check that the system is not overheating; excessive heat can cause input devices to fail.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen and trackball are unresponsive during imaging session.
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen calibration and trackball inputs failed; power cycle and recalibration did not restore functionality.
CCR.Resolution: System removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for vendor evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds, heat, or error messages
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Philips Affiniti Series Ultrasound System - Network or PACS Export Failure
description: Addresses failures sending studies to PACS or network due to configuration, connectivity, or software issues before assuming hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient depends on immediate image transfer. Move the patient to another system if urgent imaging is required.

steps.title: Check Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Verify the system is connected to the hospital network (LAN/Wi-Fi). Check cable integrity and switch ports. Expected outcome: Network link light active; system can ping hospital servers.

steps.title: Verify PACS/DICOM Configuration
steps.instructions: Ensure AE Title, IP address, and port match the PACS server settings. Check for recent changes in PACS or network policies. Expected outcome: Configuration matches PACS, no typos or mismatches.

steps.title: Test DICOM Communication
steps.instructions: Use the system’s DICOM test tool to verify connectivity to the PACS server. Expected outcome: Successful connection test; if failed, note error code.

steps.title: Check System Software Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the ultrasound software is up to date. Restart the system to clear temporary software locks or network glitches. Expected outcome: System restarts cleanly, connectivity restored.

steps.title: Review Firewall or Security Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm network firewalls or antivirus are not blocking DICOM ports (typically 104, 105, or configured custom port). Expected outcome: DICOM traffic allowed through the network.

steps.title: Try Alternate PACS Node
steps.instructions: If available, configure export to a different PACS node or workstation. Expected outcome: If export works, original PACS server settings or network path may be the issue.

CCR.Complaint: PACS export fails; images not appearing on server.
CCR.Cause: Network configuration mismatch and temporary software lock detected.
CCR.Resolution: Verified network connection, corrected PACS settings, restarted system; successful export confirmed.

helpfulDetails: Indicator lights on network port observed.
Final device status: Export verified functional.

title: Philips Allura Xper Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Generator Fault or Exposure Error
description: Identifies external causes of generator faults or exposure errors to prevent procedure interruptions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Prevents accidental radiation exposure during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check System Power and Electrical Connections
steps.instructions: Verify the main power switch is ON, circuit breakers are engaged, and power cables are securely connected. Stable power to the generator; fault indicators should reset.

steps.title: Inspect Exposure Interlocks and Safety Switches
steps.instructions: Check table, collimator, and cabinet interlocks; ensure all doors/panels are closed properly. Open interlocks prevent generator firing to protect patients.

steps.title: Review Exposure Settings and Protocols
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct imaging protocol is selected, with appropriate kVp/mA ranges. Generator accepts exposure commands without fault.

steps.title: Check System Error Messages or Logs
steps.instructions: Access the system’s service or error log to identify the specific fault code. Helps distinguish user input errors from hardware issues.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reset or Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Power down the system, wait 60 seconds, and restart. Temporary software or communication errors may clear.

steps.title: Swap or Inspect Accessories if Applicable
steps.instructions: For systems with removable cables or detectors, ensure connectors are fully seated and undamaged. Loose or damaged connections can cause generator errors.

CCR.Complaint: Generator fault displayed during routine procedure, exposure not possible.
CCR.Cause: Exposure interlock engaged due to partially open table panel; power cycle cleared temporary software fault.
CCR.Resolution: Verified interlocks and connections, performed system reset, exposure restored successfully; no further faults observed.

title: Philips Allura Xper Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Image Intensifier or Flat Panel Artifacts
description: Identifies external causes of image artifacts, ensuring clear fluoroscopic imaging and patient safety.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Prevents accidental exposure during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect Display and Monitor Settings
steps.instructions: Check for correct image mode, brightness, contrast, and active presets. Image settings match clinical protocol; some artifacts may resolve with adjustments.

steps.title: Verify Detector and Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect flat panel or image intensifier connections for looseness or damage. Secure connections may eliminate intermittent artifacts.

steps.title: Check for External Interference Sources
steps.instructions: Remove or power down nearby devices that may emit electromagnetic interference. Artifacts caused by interference should clear.

steps.title: Inspect Collimator and Filters
steps.instructions: Ensure collimator blades move freely and filters are properly seated. Misaligned collimators can produce shading or lines on images.

steps.title: Perform System Reboot
steps.instructions: Power down the system following proper shutdown procedure, then restart. Temporary software glitches causing artifacts may be resolved.

CCR.Complaint: Fluoroscopic images show horizontal lines and shading artifacts.
CCR.Cause: Flat panel detector cable found partially loose; no external interference detected.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected detector cable; performed system restart; artifacts resolved.

helpfulDetails: Check for loose cables or connectors
Record specific artifact pattern and location
Note if software updates were pending

title: Philips Allura Xper Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Table Movement Failure
description: Identifies external causes of table movement failure, such as power issues, interlocks, or mechanical obstructions, ensuring safe and functional patient positioning.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Why: Prevents injury during table movement tests.

steps.title: Check Table Power and Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Verify table is connected to a live power outlet and circuit breakers are on. Check all table power cables for secure connections. Expected outcome: Table electronics are powered and ready.

steps.title: Inspect Emergency Stop and Interlocks
steps.instructions: Ensure emergency stop buttons are released and safety interlocks (side rails, foot switches) are disengaged. Expected outcome: Table is free to respond to movement commands.

steps.title: Test Table Control Panel and Foot Switches
steps.instructions: Operate table using both the main control panel and foot switch if available. Expected outcome: Table responds to movement commands. If not, note which controls fail.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect rails, casters, and underside of the table for debris or foreign objects. Ensure cables under the table are not pinched. Expected outcome: No physical blockages preventing movement.

steps.title: Verify System Software Status
steps.instructions: Check for system warnings or software errors that may inhibit table movement. Reboot the system if safe and permitted by protocol. Expected outcome: Software allows normal table operation.

CCR.Complaint: Table fails to move during standard positioning commands.
CCR.Cause: Emergency stop engaged and minor debris obstructing underside of table.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared obstruction, released emergency stop, and verified table movement via control panel and foot switch.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual resistance or binding during manual movement

title: Philips Allura Xper Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Software Boot or Initialization Errors
description: Identifies external causes of software boot or initialization failures, such as power instability, network issues, or peripheral conflicts, ensuring safe and timely system readiness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Why: Prevents accidental exposure or injury during system startup.

steps.title: Check Power Supply
steps.instructions: Verify the system is connected to a stable, active outlet and UPS (if used) is functioning. Expected Outcome: System receives consistent power; no fault indicators on main panel.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all network, peripheral, and internal communication cables are seated properly. Why: Loose or damaged connections can prevent successful boot.

steps.title: Verify External Devices
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential peripherals (USB drives, external monitors, network devices). Expected Outcome: System should attempt boot without peripheral conflicts.

steps.title: Observe Boot Sequence
steps.instructions: Power on system and note any error codes, beeps, or abnormal delays. Why: Provides diagnostic clues for escalation or service documentation.

steps.title: Check Network and Server Access
steps.instructions: Ensure the system can reach PACS/DICOM servers if initialization depends on network verification. Expected Outcome: System proceeds to login or ready state without network timeouts.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reset
steps.instructions: Use manufacturer-recommended soft reset procedure if safe. Expected Outcome: System completes boot successfully; software initializes properly.

CCR.Complaint: System fails to boot, displays initialization errors at startup.
CCR.Cause: Power fluctuations and peripheral conflict prevented software initialization.
CCR.Resolution: Verified stable power, disconnected nonessential peripherals, performed soft reset; boot successful.

title: Philips Allura Xper Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - DICOM / PACS Transfer Failure
description: Identifies external causes of DICOM or PACS transfer failure, including network issues, server connectivity, or configuration errors, ensuring timely image availability for patient care.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is actively undergoing a procedure while troubleshooting network or system issues. Why: Prevents disruption to live imaging and ensures staff are clear of equipment.

steps.title: Check System Power and Status
steps.instructions: Verify the Allura Xper is powered on with normal system boot indicators. Expected Outcome: System is ready; no error lights or boot failures. Why: A device not fully booted cannot communicate with PACS.

steps.title: Verify Network Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect Ethernet cables, switches, and wall ports for secure connections. Test network link lights or use a network tester if available. Expected Outcome: Active network link with stable connectivity. Why: DICOM relies on a stable network to reach PACS servers.

steps.title: Ping PACS / DICOM Server
steps.instructions: From the Allura console or associated workstation, ping the PACS server IP. Expected Outcome: Successful response indicates network and server reachability. Why: Confirms that the server is accessible and routing is correct.

steps.title: Check DICOM Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify AE titles, IP addresses, and ports match PACS server settings. Ensure DICOM service is enabled on both the Allura system and PACS. Expected Outcome: Properly configured DICOM connection. Why: Misconfigured AE titles or addresses are common causes of transfer failure.

steps.title: Inspect PACS Server and Storage
steps.instructions: Confirm server is operational, has storage space, and is not rejecting incoming studies. Expected Outcome: PACS server accepts new studies. Why: Transfer can fail if the server is offline or full.

steps.title: Test with a Small Study
steps.instructions: Send a test image or study to PACS to verify functionality. Expected Outcome: Test study successfully received in PACS. Why: Confirms transfer capability without involving live patient procedures.

CCR.Complaint: Cath lab staff reported failure to send images to PACS from Allura Xper.
CCR.Cause: Network misconfiguration and unreachable PACS server.
CCR.Resolution: Verified cables, corrected DICOM settings, and confirmed successful test transfer.

helpfulDetails: No abnormal lights or alarms on Allura Xper

title: Philips Azurion Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Generator Fault or Exposure Error
description: Identifies external causes of generator faults or exposure errors, ensuring safe and uninterrupted procedures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Prevents injury from unexpected exposure or table movement.

steps.title: Verify Main Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the system is plugged into a dedicated outlet with correct voltage. Inspect circuit breakers and UPS (if present). Expected Outcome: System powers on without error.

steps.title: Check System Interlocks
steps.instructions: Inspect all doors, panels, and footswitches for proper closure and function. Expected Outcome: Interlocks engaged, no error indicators lit.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Verify high-voltage, generator, and control cables are secure and undamaged. Expected Outcome: No loose or damaged connections causing fault.

steps.title: Review Exposure Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm appropriate kV, mA, and dose settings for the procedure. Expected Outcome: Settings within acceptable range; no exposure error.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled shutdown and restart. Expected Outcome: Error resolves; system ready for use.

CCR.Complaint: Generator fault displayed during procedure initiation.
CCR.Cause: Power interruption detected at main supply; exposure settings outside recommended range.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected outlet issue and reset exposure parameters; system tested successfully.

helpfulDetails: Indicator lights and alarm behavior observed

title: Philips Azurion Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Flat Panel or Detector Artifacts
description: Identifies external causes of flat panel or detector artifacts, ensuring clear imaging and patient safety.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Why: Prevents injury during equipment handling or adjustment.

steps.title: Inspect Detector Connections
steps.instructions: Check all flat panel and detector cables for secure connections and visible damage. Expected Outcome: Properly seated cables may eliminate intermittent or patterned artifacts.

steps.title: Verify System Power Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure system is plugged into a stable power source; check UPS or backup systems. Expected Outcome: Power fluctuations can create transient artifacts; stable power resolves these.

steps.title: Check for Interference Sources
steps.instructions: Remove or turn off nearby electronic devices that may emit electromagnetic interference (EMI). Expected Outcome: Artifact patterns caused by EMI should disappear.

steps.title: Inspect Detector Surface and Housing
steps.instructions: Look for dirt, smudges, or obstructions on the detector surface; clean per manufacturer guidelines. Expected Outcome: Cleaning may remove streaks or localized bright/dark spots.

steps.title: Review Imaging Parameters and Acquisition Settings
steps.instructions: Verify correct acquisition mode, dose settings, and image post-processing settings. Expected Outcome: Incorrect parameters can create apparent artifacts; correcting settings resolves them.

steps.title: Perform System Self-Test / Detector Calibration
steps.instructions: Run detector calibration or self-test routines per system prompts. Expected Outcome: Detector artifacts due to misalignment or calibration drift may be corrected.

CCR.Complaint: Interventional staff reported streaking and bright spots on flat panel images during procedures.
CCR.Cause: Loose detector cable and minor calibration drift identified.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected cable, cleaned detector surface, and ran calibration; artifacts resolved.

title: Philips Azurion Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Table Movement Failure
description: Identifies external causes of table movement failure, including power issues, interlocks, or mechanical obstructions, ensuring safe patient positioning and workflow continuity.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Why: Prevents injury during movement or troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the table power switch is ON. Confirm the table is connected to a live outlet with no tripped breakers. Expected outcome: Table lights and indicators should be active.

steps.title: Check Foot and Hand Controls
steps.instructions: Ensure pedals and hand controls are properly seated and functioning. Test movement in all directions. Why: Faulty control interface can mimic movement failure.

steps.title: Inspect Interlocks and Safety Mechanisms
steps.instructions: Verify that all mechanical locks are disengaged (e.g., brakes, side rails, accessory locks). Confirm no ancillary equipment is obstructing movement. Expected outcome: Table should respond when locks are disengaged.

steps.title: Examine Mechanical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Check under and around the table for cables, tubing, or objects preventing movement. Why: Even small obstacles can prevent table motors from engaging.

steps.title: Reset Table Electronics
steps.instructions: Power-cycle the table by turning OFF, waiting 30 seconds, and turning ON. Expected outcome: Table control system should initialize and allow movement.

steps.title: Swap Control Cables if Applicable
steps.instructions: If controls are modular, swap cables to rule out damaged wiring. Expected outcome: Table responds correctly with alternate control cable.

CCR.Complaint: Table will not move in any direction during procedures.
CCR.Cause: Mechanical brake was engaged, and control cable showed intermittent connection.
CCR.Resolution: Brake released, cable reseated, table successfully powered and tested.

helpfulDetails: Indicator lights on table control panel

title: Philips Azurion Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - Software Boot or Initialization Errors
description: Identifies external causes of system boot or initialization failures, including power instability, network issues, and peripheral conflicts, ensuring safe and timely system readiness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table and staff are clear. Why: Prevents injury or interruption during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Main Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the system is connected to a reliable outlet and power is stable. Confirm the emergency power off (EPO) switch is disengaged. Expected Outcome: System receives proper voltage and ready for startup.

steps.title: Check System Power Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe LEDs or display lights on the main console and generator cabinet. Why: Indicator lights help identify power-related faults. Expected Outcome: Normal power indicators (green) confirm power is present.

steps.title: Restart the System Properly
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled power cycle using the main power switch. Expected Outcome: System attempts a full boot sequence without errors.

steps.title: Inspect Peripheral Connections
steps.instructions: Verify all network cables, detectors, and control panels are securely connected. Why: Loose or disconnected peripherals can prevent initialization. Expected Outcome: No loose connections; boot proceeds past hardware detection.

steps.title: Check Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Ensure connection to the hospital network, PACS, and license servers. Expected Outcome: System communicates with essential services without timeout errors.

steps.title: Review Recent Software or Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Confirm no updates, patches, or configuration changes were applied recently. Why: Conflicting software or incomplete updates can prevent boot. Expected Outcome: System boots with default or known good configuration.

steps.title: Escalate if Boot Fails
steps.instructions: If the system fails to initialize after these steps: Remove the device from service. Label “Out of Service.” Send for vendor support or bench evaluation. Why: Internal software or hardware fault likely; do not attempt board-level repair.

CCR.Complaint: System fails to boot or initialize; displays error messages on startup.
CCR.Cause: Loose network and peripheral connections discovered during inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Secured connections, performed controlled power cycle, system successfully booted.

helpfulDetails: All network and detector cables secured
System boot logs reviewed for errors

title: Philips Azurion Fluoroscopy / Interventional System - DICOM / PACS Transfer Failure
description: Identifies external causes of DICOM/PACS transfer failures, including network connectivity, server access, and configuration issues, ensuring timely image availability for patient care.

steps.title: Ensure Patient and Staff Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is actively undergoing a procedure. Why: Prevents interruption of patient care and avoids accidental system interaction.

steps.title: Check Network Connections
steps.instructions: Verify Ethernet cables are securely connected to the Azurion and network switches. Expected outcome: No loose or disconnected cables.

steps.title: Confirm Network Activity
steps.instructions: Check the system’s network status indicators and test ping to the PACS server. Expected outcome: Successful response from the server confirms network reachability.

steps.title: Verify PACS Server Availability
steps.instructions: Confirm PACS server is powered on and accessible from other systems. Why: System cannot transfer images if the server is offline.

steps.title: Check DICOM Configuration Settings
steps.instructions: Validate AE Titles, IP addresses, and ports match PACS server requirements. Expected outcome: Correct configuration ensures proper communication.

steps.title: Inspect Firewall or Security Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm firewalls or security appliances are not blocking DICOM ports. Why: Blocked ports prevent image transfer.

steps.title: Review System Logs
steps.instructions: Check Azurion event logs for error codes or failed transfer entries. Expected outcome: Identifies specific network or configuration errors for escalation.

steps.title: Test with Alternate Server or Workstation
steps.instructions: Attempt to transfer a test image to another PACS or workstation. Why: Confirms if issue is system-specific or network/server related.

CCR.Complaint: Images are not transferring to PACS from the Azurion.
CCR.Cause: Network connectivity verified; DICOM configuration and server availability confirmed.
CCR.Resolution: Network ports and PACS settings verified; issue escalated to service team for internal troubleshooting.

helpfulDetails: Current device status: Out of Service if escalated

title: Philips CX50 Ultrasound System - Probe Not Recognized or Connection Errors
description: Troubleshooting probe detection issues, connection errors, or inability of the system to recognize an attached ultrasound transducer.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the probe from patient use and transition to another functioning system if imaging is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Connect the probe and observe the system response. Check for messages such as “Probe Not Recognized,” “No Transducer Connected,” or intermittent connection behavior.

steps.title: Check Probe Connection and Port Seating
steps.instructions: Disconnect and firmly reconnect the probe. Ensure the connector is fully seated and locked into place.

steps.title: Inspect Probe Connector and Cable
steps.instructions: Visually inspect for bent or missing pins, cracks in connector housing, and frayed or kinked cable. Gently manipulate the cable near the connector while observing system response.

steps.title: Try a Known Working Probe
steps.instructions: Connect a different probe that is confirmed working. If new probe works, the original probe is likely faulty. If new probe fails, a system or port issue is likely.

steps.title: Test Multiple Probe Ports (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: If the CX50 has multiple ports, connect the probe to a different port.

steps.title: Check System for Debris or Damage in Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the probe port for dust or debris, bent internal pins, or fluid contamination. Do not insert objects—use visual inspection only.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Perform a full system reboot.

steps.title: Verify System Settings and Software Status
steps.instructions: Check if the system is fully booted and not in a restricted or error state. Ensure no system errors are preventing probe initialization.

CCR.Complaint: Probe not recognized when connected to Philips CX50 ultrasound system.
CCR.Cause: Faulty probe cable with intermittent connection identified during manipulation test.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defective probe from service and replaced with functional probe; confirmed normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (working / removed from service)

title: Philips CX50 Ultrasound System - Battery Not Charging or Shortened Runtime
description: Troubleshooting charging failure, rapid battery drain, or inability to maintain battery power during portable ultrasound use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the system from active patient use before troubleshooting battery or power issues. If needed, move the patient to another ultrasound system.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the system and observe battery status indicators. Check if the unit does not charge when plugged in, shows low battery despite being connected, or has significantly reduced runtime.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet using another device if needed.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the AC power cord and external power supply for loose connections, damage, and indicator light status. Reseat all connections securely.

steps.title: Confirm Charging Indicator on System
steps.instructions: Observe the CX50 charging indicator to determine if the system recognizes incoming power.

steps.title: Check Battery Seating (If Accessible)
steps.instructions: Power down the unit, remove and reseat the battery, and inspect contacts and physical condition.

steps.title: Test Runtime on Battery Only
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and operate the system on battery to evaluate runtime performance.

steps.title: Evaluate Battery Age and Usage
steps.instructions: Check maintenance records or labeling for battery age and compare against typical 2–3 year lifespan.

steps.title: Perform System Restart
steps.instructions: Shut down, disconnect from AC, wait 30 seconds, restart, and reconnect power to clear temporary faults.

CCR.Complaint: CX50 ultrasound battery not charging and losing power quickly during portable use.
CCR.Cause: Battery found to have degraded capacity with no charge retention despite proper AC power and connections.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and battery replaced; system tested and confirmed normal charging and runtime.

title: Philips CX50 Ultrasound System - Image Artifact or Quality Degradation
description: Troubleshooting poor image quality, artifacts, noise, or degraded visualization caused by probes, settings, connections, or environmental factors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If image quality is impacting diagnosis, remove the system from patient use and move to another ultrasound system. Expected: Patient care continues without diagnostic risk. Why it matters: Poor imaging can lead to misdiagnosis or missed clinical findings.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm the artifact or quality issue. Identify type (noise, dropouts, lines, shadowing, distortion). Determine if it occurs on all probes or only one. Check if it is present across all imaging modes (2D, Doppler, etc.). Expected: Issue is consistent and reproducible. Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the problem is probe-specific or system-wide.

steps.title: Check Probe Condition and Selection
steps.instructions: Inspect the active probe. Look for cracks, delamination, or fluid intrusion. Check cable for kinks or exposed wiring. Ensure correct probe is selected in the system. Swap with a known-good probe if available. Expected: Image improves with alternate probe if original is faulty. Why it matters: Probes are the most common cause of image artifacts.

steps.title: Inspect Probe Connection and Port
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the probe securely. Inspect connector pins for damage or debris. Try a different probe port if available. Expected: Stable connection with no intermittent signal loss. Why it matters: Poor connections can cause signal dropouts or noise.

steps.title: Check Imaging Settings
steps.instructions: Review and adjust key settings. Gain (too high = noise, too low = poor visibility). Depth and focus alignment. Frequency selection. TGC (Time Gain Compensation) sliders. Preset selection (correct exam type). Reset to default preset if needed. Expected: Image clarity improves with proper optimization. Why it matters: Incorrect settings can mimic hardware failure.

steps.title: Evaluate Environmental Interference
steps.instructions: Check for nearby sources of interference. Other electrical equipment. Mobile devices or wireless transmitters. Poor grounding or shared outlets. Move the system to another outlet if needed. Expected: Reduction in noise or artifact. Why it matters: Electrical interference can degrade ultrasound signals.

steps.title: Perform System Restart
steps.instructions: Power down the system completely and restart. Expected: Temporary software-related artifacts may clear. Why it matters: Software glitches can affect image processing.

steps.title: Check System Performance Across Modes
steps.instructions: Test multiple imaging modes. 2D imaging. Color Doppler. M-mode. Expected: Issue may appear in specific modes only. Why it matters: Helps determine if problem is processing-related vs hardware.
