Expected outcome: A simple test object should produce a clear and stable image. If the test image is good, the issue may have been patient size, positioning, technique, or procedure setup.

steps.title: Check Imaging Mode and Dose Settings
steps.instructions: Incorrect imaging mode can make the image look poor. Verify the selected imaging mode is appropriate. Check whether low-dose fluoro, pulsed fluoro, snapshot, or another mode is selected. Confirm clinical staff are not expecting high-detail imaging while the unit is in a lower-dose mode. Expected outcome: Image quality should match the selected mode. If changing to an appropriate mode resolves the complaint, document the setting issue and return the unit to service.

steps.title: Check Automatic Brightness or Exposure Behavior
steps.instructions: Automatic exposure control problems can appear as dark, bright, or unstable images. Observe whether image brightness adjusts normally when the field changes. Move a test object into and out of the field while watching the image response. Look for brightness pumping, delayed adjustment, or failure to compensate. Expected outcome: The system should adjust image brightness smoothly and predictably. If brightness control is unstable or does not respond, remove the unit from service for further evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the Image Intensifier Face and External Covers
steps.instructions: External contamination or damage can affect image appearance. Inspect the image intensifier input area for visible debris, cracks, fluid, residue, or damage. Check for physical impact damage to the C-arm head or image intensifier housing. Do not remove covers or open the image intensifier assembly during floor troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The image intensifier area should be clean, intact, and free of obvious damage. If contamination is present, clean according to facility-approved procedures. If damage is present, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Check Cable Connections Between the C-Arm and Workstation
steps.instructions: A poor connection can cause noisy, intermittent, or distorted images. Inspect external video, control, and system cables between the C-arm and monitor/workstation cart if applicable. Check for loose connectors, damaged cable jackets, bent pins, stretched cables, or strain at connection points. Reseat accessible external connectors only if safe and allowed by local procedure. Expected outcome: Cables should be secure, undamaged, and properly connected. If reseating a cable resolves the issue, perform functional testing before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Image Changes With Movement
steps.instructions: C-arm movement can reveal cable or connection issues. While imaging a test object, carefully move the C-arm through common positions. Observe whether the image flickers, distorts, drops out, becomes noisy, or changes brightness. Do not force movement or continue testing if cables appear strained. Expected outcome: Image quality should remain stable through normal positioning. If image quality changes with movement, suspect cable strain, connector issue, or internal connection problem. Remove from service for bench or vendor evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or System Warnings
steps.instructions: Image quality issues may be accompanied by system messages. Review the system display for warnings, camera errors, generator errors, imaging chain faults, or startup messages. Record the exact wording of any message. Power cycle the system only if it is not in active clinical use and it is safe to do so. Expected outcome: No active imaging-related errors should remain after startup. If errors return, remove the unit from service and escalate.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Imaging Check
steps.instructions: Confirm the system can produce a usable image under controlled conditions. Use a known test object. Verify image appears centered, brightness is acceptable, contrast is acceptable, no major artifacts are present, image is stable, both monitors display correctly, and image storage or transfer works if part of the complaint. Expected outcome: The BV Pulsera should produce a stable and clinically usable image. If image quality remains poor after external checks, do not continue clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported poor image quality on the Philips BV Pulsera, with the fluoroscopy image appearing blurry and low contrast during setup.
CCR.Cause: External checks found the image intensifier positioned too far from the test object and a metal table rail partially in the beam path.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned the C-arm, cleared the beam path, verified acceptable image quality on both monitors, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Dose mode or imaging mode selected
Artifacts, distortion, lag, or noise observed
Unusual sounds, heat, odor, or physical damage

title: Philips BV Pulsera C-Arm - Workstation / Monitor Cart Communication Problem
description: Troubleshooting communication loss between the BV Pulsera C-arm and workstation/monitor cart using safe external checks first.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot the system during an active procedure unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Confirm whether the C-arm is currently being used for patient imaging. If the issue occurs during a case, notify clinical staff immediately. If imaging is needed urgently, have staff move to another available C-arm if possible. Do not repeatedly power-cycle the system during an active procedure without clinical approval. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before equipment troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A communication problem between the C-arm and monitor cart can affect image display, image transfer, system control, and procedural workflow.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is failing. Ask staff what they observed: Monitor cart not displaying image, Workstation not responding, C-arm not detected by workstation, Live image not appearing, Stored images unavailable, Error message related to communication, Intermittent disconnect between stand and cart. Check whether the C-arm powers up normally and whether the monitor cart powers up normally. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to communication between the C-arm stand and monitor/workstation cart, not a total power failure. If the issue is actually no power to either the C-arm or cart, troubleshoot power first and stop this guide.

steps.title: Check AC Power to Both the C-Arm and Monitor Cart
steps.instructions: Verify both parts of the system are powered correctly. Confirm the C-arm stand is plugged into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the monitor/workstation cart is plugged in if it uses separate AC power. Check that power cords are fully seated and not damaged. Verify outlet power using an outlet tester or another known-good device if needed. Look for tripped breakers, loose wall receptacles, or switched outlets. Expected outcome: Both the C-arm and monitor cart receive stable AC power. If power is restored and communication returns, document the outlet or power connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Communication Cable Between the C-Arm and Cart
steps.instructions: Check the external cable path between the C-arm stand and monitor/workstation cart. Locate the communication/interface cable between the C-arm and monitor cart. Inspect both ends for loose connections. Reseat the cable at both the C-arm and cart ends. Check for bent pins, damaged connectors, broken locking hardware, crushed cable jacket, or strain damage. Verify the cable is routed safely and not pinched under wheels, doors, pedals, or cart movement points. Expected outcome: The communication cable is fully seated and visibly undamaged. If reseating the cable restores communication, perform a functional check and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Cart Is Connected to the Correct C-Arm
steps.instructions: Verify that the monitor cart belongs with the BV Pulsera system being used. Check asset tags, serial numbers, labels, or department markings. Confirm the cart has not been swapped with another Philips C-arm system. Ask staff whether carts or stands were moved recently. Expected outcome: The correct monitor/workstation cart is connected to the correct C-arm stand. Why it matters: C-arm systems are often moved between rooms, and mismatched carts can create communication or configuration problems. If a cart mismatch is found, reconnect the correct components, test the system, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Damage From Movement
steps.instructions: Inspect for obvious damage caused by transport or repositioning. Look at the cable connectors, cart docking area, cable strain reliefs, monitor cart base, and rear connection panel. Check whether the system was recently moved through doorways, elevators, or tight hallways. Look for impact marks, cracked housings, loose panels, damaged wheels, or signs that a cable was pulled. Expected outcome: No visible transport-related damage is found. If damage is found, remove the system from service and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Power Down and Restart the System Safely
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled restart only when it is safe to interrupt imaging. Confirm with clinical staff that the system is not needed for an active patient. Power down the C-arm and monitor/workstation cart using the normal shutdown process. Wait long enough for the system to fully shut down. Power the system back on in the normal sequence used by the site. Observe startup behavior and check for communication errors. Expected outcome: The workstation and C-arm re-establish communication after restart. If the system communicates normally after restart, perform a basic functional check and document the software/communication reset.

steps.title: Check the Monitor Cart Display and Workstation Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the cart is powered but not communicating, or if the cart itself is malfunctioning. Check whether the monitors turn on. Check whether the workstation computer boots normally. Look for frozen screens, blank displays, startup errors, keyboard/mouse issues, or application lockups. Confirm whether any live fluoroscopy image, stored image, or system status information is displayed. Expected outcome: The monitor cart boots normally and only the C-arm communication path remains suspect. If the cart does not boot, freezes, or has no display output, troubleshoot the monitor cart/workstation power or computer issue separately.

steps.title: Check External Accessories and Connections
steps.instructions: Verify that accessories are not creating confusion with the reported problem. Inspect footswitches, hand controls, keyboard, mouse, image storage devices, printer connections, and network cables if present. Disconnect non-essential external accessories only if approved by site policy and safe to do so. Do not disconnect required imaging or safety-related connections during clinical use. Expected outcome: No external accessory or loose peripheral connection is causing the reported workstation problem. If removing or reseating a non-essential accessory restores normal operation, document the accessory connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Whether the Problem Is Intermittent
steps.instructions: Try to determine if the failure happens during movement or only at startup. With the system safely out of patient use, gently observe whether communication drops when the cart or cable is moved. Do not stress, bend, or pull cables aggressively. Check whether the issue occurs after moving the system, turning the cart, locking wheels, or repositioning the C-arm. Expected outcome: The issue is either stable or linked to movement/cable position. If communication drops with movement, suspect cable, connector, strain relief, or internal connection damage. Remove the system from service.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check After Communication Returns
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is safe to return to service. Verify the system boots without communication errors. Confirm the monitor cart displays the expected system interface. Confirm live image display is available using the site-approved test method. Confirm image storage/review functions are available if normally used. Check for unusual noises, heat, smell, flickering displays, or recurring error messages. Expected outcome: The BV Pulsera communicates normally with the workstation/monitor cart and passes a basic operational check. If the system passes, return it to service per site policy.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips BV Pulsera monitor cart was not communicating with the C-arm and no live image was displaying on the workstation.
CCR.Cause: Found the communication cable between the C-arm stand and monitor cart partially seated at the cart connection.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the communication cable, restarted the system, verified live image display and workstation response, and returned the C-arm to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the cart monitors were blank, frozen, or working
Cart and C-arm asset tags verified
Issue occurred after transport or room move
Unusual sounds, heat, smell, or display flicker

title: Philips BV Pulsera C-Arm - DICOM / Network Transfer Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed DICOM send, PACS transfer, worklist, or network communication issues on a Philips BV Pulsera C-arm.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot network transfers during an active procedure unless image availability has been protected. Action: Confirm the procedure is complete or paused safely. Verify that needed images are still available locally on the BV Pulsera. Confirm with clinical staff whether images are urgently needed for patient care. If images are required immediately, use an approved alternate export method if available, such as CD/DVD, USB, or local print, depending on site policy. Expected outcome: Patient care is not delayed while the network issue is investigated. Why it matters: A DICOM failure may not affect live fluoroscopy, but it can delay image review, documentation, surgical follow-up, or PACS availability.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is failing. Action: Ask clinical staff what they were trying to do when the issue occurred. Determine whether the problem involves DICOM image send, PACS transfer, modality worklist, patient query, network storage destination, or printer transfer. Check whether the system gives an error message, timeout, failed queue, or no response. Expected outcome: The failure type is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues. If the issue was a one-time failed send and a retry completes successfully, document the event and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Images Are Stored Locally
steps.instructions: Before making changes, confirm the images are still present on the BV Pulsera. Action: Open the local patient or study list. Verify that the expected images or runs are still available. Do not delete, overwrite, or clear patient data during troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The study remains available locally and can be resent after the issue is corrected. Why it matters: The first priority is preserving the acquired images.

steps.title: Check the Physical Network Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the basic network path first. Action: Verify the Ethernet cable is connected securely to the C-arm workstation or network connection point. Check the wall jack, network drop, patch cable, and any cart-mounted network connection. Look for damaged cable ends, loose connectors, broken locking tabs, or cables pulled tight during room movement. If available, try a known-good network cable and known-good wall jack approved for that imaging device. Expected outcome: The system has a stable physical network connection. If reseating or replacing the cable restores transfer, resend the study, confirm receipt in PACS, document the correction, and stop.

steps.title: Check Link Lights and Network Activity
steps.instructions: Look for signs that the network port is active. Action: Check for link/activity lights at the network port if visible. Compare the behavior to a nearby known-working network connection if possible. If there are no link lights, test the cable and jack with a known-good device or request IT network verification. Expected outcome: The network port shows an active connection. If no link is present after cable and jack checks, the issue is likely network drop, switch port, cable, or internal network hardware related.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Network Location Is Being Used
steps.instructions: C-arms are often moved between rooms, and not every wall jack may be configured for imaging network traffic. Action: Ask whether the BV Pulsera was recently moved. Verify it is connected to the correct network jack for that procedure area. Confirm with IT or imaging support that the jack is patched to the correct VLAN or network segment for DICOM/PACS communication. Expected outcome: The C-arm is connected to a network path intended for imaging transfers. If connecting to the correct jack restores transfer, resend the study, verify receipt, document, and stop.

steps.title: Check System Network Status
steps.instructions: Review the system’s network status if available from the user-accessible service or configuration area. Action: Confirm the system shows a valid network connection. Check whether the system has an IP address. Look for signs of duplicate IP, missing IP, disconnected network, or communication timeout. Do not change IP, AE Title, port, gateway, or destination settings unless authorized by site process. Expected outcome: The BV Pulsera has valid network status and is not showing a basic connection failure. Why it matters: Incorrect network settings can break PACS transfer, worklist, and storage communication.

steps.title: Verify DICOM Destination Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm the user is sending to the correct destination. Action: Check whether multiple destinations are listed. Verify the selected destination matches the intended PACS, archive, review station, or DICOM printer. Ask whether other C-arms or imaging systems are currently sending successfully to the same destination. Expected outcome: The correct DICOM destination is selected. If the wrong destination was selected, choose the correct one, retry the transfer, confirm receipt, document, and stop.

steps.title: Check the Transfer Queue
steps.instructions: A failed or stuck queue can prevent new studies from sending. Action: Review the DICOM transfer queue if accessible. Look for failed, pending, retrying, or stuck studies. Attempt a resend of the failed study if appropriate. Do not delete queued studies unless clinical staff confirms they are no longer needed and site policy allows it. Expected outcome: The failed transfer is retried or the queue behavior is confirmed. If the resend is successful and PACS confirms receipt, document the successful transfer and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known Good Study or Test Send
steps.instructions: Use a controlled test when patient images are not at risk. Action: If allowed by site policy, send a test study or non-critical study to the configured destination. Confirm whether the transfer fails immediately, times out, or completes. Compare with another known-working modality on the same network if available. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to the BV Pulsera, the network path, or the receiving system. Why it matters: A transfer failure may be caused by the C-arm, network infrastructure, PACS, DICOM router, or destination storage.

steps.title: Check Whether PACS or DICOM Services Are Available
steps.instructions: The C-arm may be working correctly while the receiving system is unavailable. Action: Contact PACS, radiology IT, or imaging informatics. Ask whether PACS, DICOM router, modality worklist, or storage services are currently down or degraded. Ask whether other modalities are experiencing the same issue. Expected outcome: External system availability is confirmed. If PACS or the DICOM router is down, leave the study stored locally, notify clinical staff, document the external outage, and stop device repair troubleshooting.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the system appears locked, queue behavior is abnormal, or network communication does not recover, perform a controlled restart only when it is safe. Action: Confirm the procedure is complete and images are stored. Follow normal shutdown procedures. Restart the BV Pulsera workstation/system. Allow the system to fully boot and reconnect to the network. Retry the transfer. Expected outcome: Temporary software or network session issues clear after restart. If the transfer completes after restart, confirm receipt in PACS, document the action, and stop.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Widespread Failures
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is isolated or recurring. Action: Review recent user reports. Ask if the failure happens in one room, one wall jack, one destination, one user workflow, or every transfer attempt. Check whether the issue follows the C-arm when moved to another approved network location. Expected outcome: The pattern helps separate device failure from network or PACS infrastructure problems. If the issue follows the C-arm across known-good network drops, the device requires further evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Philips BV Pulsera C-arm would not send procedure images to PACS after the case.
CCR.Cause: Found Ethernet cable connected to an inactive wall jack, preventing DICOM communication with the PACS destination.
CCR.Resolution: Connected the system to the approved imaging network jack, resent the study successfully, and confirmed image receipt with radiology/PACS staff.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, exact error message, timeout behavior, failed queue status, or no response condition
Accessories swapped, including Ethernet cable, network drop, wall jack, patch cable, or cart-mounted connection
Power behavior, restart results, boot status, network reconnect status, and whether the transfer worked after restart
Environmental factors, including outlet or room location used, whether the C-arm was recently moved, and whether other modalities were affected
Indicator lights, including link/activity light status at the network port if visible

Final device status, including whether images remained stored locally, whether study resend was successful, PACS or IT confirmation, and whether the device was returned to service or labeled Out of Service

title: Philips NM3 Capnography Monitor - NO CO₂ WAVEFORM OR INACCURATE EtCO₂ READING
description: Troubleshooting missing CO₂ waveform, unstable capnogram, or inaccurate EtCO₂ readings caused by setup, sampling, patient connection, or module issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Philips NM3 is actively monitoring a patient, verify that clinical staff are aware of the missing or questionable CO₂ reading. If EtCO₂ monitoring is clinically required, move the patient to another working capnography monitor or approved monitoring method before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient ventilation monitoring continues safely. If this resolves the immediate patient safety concern, continue troubleshooting only when safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is: No CO₂ waveform; Flat waveform; Intermittent waveform; EtCO₂ value missing; EtCO₂ value present but inaccurate; Delayed response; Frequent occlusion, sampling, or calibration-related messages. Expected outcome: The exact symptom is confirmed before parts are replaced or the device is removed from service.

steps.title: Check the Patient and Airway Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the sampling line, airway adapter, cannula, or nasal/oral sampling device is properly connected to the patient circuit or patient interface. Look for disconnection, loose fittings, reversed adapters, or sampling ports not exposed to exhaled gas. Expected outcome: CO₂ waveform appears and EtCO₂ value returns to expected monitoring behavior. If corrected, document the connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the CO₂ Sampling Line or Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the sampling line for: Kinks; Crushed tubing; Moisture; Secretions; Blood; Condensation; Blockage; Loose luer connection; Incorrect accessory type. Replace the sampling line or airway adapter with a known-good compatible accessory. Expected outcome: A blocked or defective sampling accessory is ruled out or corrected. If the waveform returns after replacing the accessory, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Accessory Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the accessory is appropriate for the Philips NM3 and the type of monitoring being performed. Confirm the setup matches the clinical application, such as intubated, non-intubated, sidestream, or sampling-line monitoring. Expected outcome: The monitor receives a valid respiratory gas sample. Why it matters: Incorrect or incompatible accessories can cause weak waveforms, inaccurate EtCO₂ readings, or no breath detection.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Contamination at the Module Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the module’s sampling inlet and the sampling line connector area for visible fluid, contamination, or damaged fittings. Do not force tubing into a damaged port. Do not attempt deep internal cleaning. Expected outcome: External contamination or connection damage is identified. If fluid contamination is suspected inside the module, remove the device or module from service.

steps.title: Verify Monitor and Module Recognition
steps.instructions: Confirm the Philips monitor recognizes the NM3 module and displays CO₂ parameters. Check for module-related messages, missing parameter boxes, communication errors, or startup failures. Remove and reseat the module only if safe and appropriate for the setup. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the module and CO₂ monitoring becomes available. If the module is not recognized after reseating, remove it from service for evaluation.

steps.title: Check Alarm, Scale, and Display Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm CO₂ monitoring is enabled and that the waveform is not hidden, minimized, or scaled in a way that makes it appear flat. Review EtCO₂ alarm limits, waveform display settings, and parameter configuration. Expected outcome: A display or configuration issue is ruled out. If the waveform was hidden or misconfigured, correct the setting, verify operation, and stop.

steps.title: Compare Against a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: If available, test the same patient setup with a known-good CO₂ module, sampling line, or monitor. Alternatively, test the NM3 module on a compatible known-good monitor if allowed by site practice. Expected outcome: The issue follows either the accessory, patient setup, monitor, or NM3 module. Why it matters: Swapping known-good external components helps avoid unnecessary module replacement.

steps.title: Check for Patient or Clinical Factors
steps.instructions: If the waveform exists but the EtCO₂ value appears low, high, or inconsistent, confirm clinical staff have evaluated the patient and ventilation status. Possible non-equipment causes may include shallow breathing, poor sampling position, mouth breathing with nasal cannula, leak around the airway connection, low tidal volume, or ventilation changes. Expected outcome: Equipment troubleshooting does not override clinical assessment.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check Off Patient
steps.instructions: Using site-approved test equipment, simulator, or known-good CO₂ source, verify whether the NM3 can detect CO₂ and display a stable waveform/value. Do not use the device for patient care if the reading cannot be verified. Expected outcome: CO₂ function is confirmed or the fault is reproduced off patient.

steps.title: Review Recent Service, Cleaning, or Damage History
steps.instructions: Check whether the module was recently dropped, cleaned, exposed to fluid, moved between monitors, or returned from a clinical area with accessory contamination. Expected outcome: Recent handling or environmental exposure is considered before declaring a random failure.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported no CO₂ waveform and no reliable EtCO₂ reading from the Philips NM3 during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Found the CO₂ sampling line kinked near the patient connection, preventing proper gas sampling.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the sampling line, verified waveform and EtCO₂ returned, and confirmed the monitor operated normally before returning to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient was moved to another monitor
Exact symptom: no waveform, flat waveform, unstable waveform, or inaccurate value
Any error or status message displayed

title: Philips NM3 Capnography Monitor - Sampling Line Blocked, Leaking, Kinked, or Disconnected
description: Troubleshooting CO2 sampling issues caused by blocked, leaking, kinked, disconnected, or improperly installed sampling lines.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Philips NM3 is actively monitoring a patient, confirm that clinical staff are aware of the CO2 monitoring issue. If EtCO2, respiration rate, or apnea monitoring is clinically required, move the patient to another working monitor or alternate approved monitoring method before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient ventilation monitoring remains continuous and safe. Why it matters: A sampling line problem can prevent accurate EtCO2 readings and may delay recognition of respiratory compromise.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact issue reported by staff. Look for symptoms such as: No EtCO2 value; Low or unstable EtCO2 readings; No CO2 waveform; Intermittent waveform; Sampling line blocked message; Sampling line disconnected or leak-related message; Pump noise or reduced sampling flow. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed before parts are replaced or the monitor is removed from service. If the issue is not present during testing, document that the reported condition could not be duplicated and continue checking external causes.

steps.title: Inspect the Sampling Line Connection at the Monitor
steps.instructions: Check that the sampling line is fully seated in the NM3 CO2 inlet connection. Remove and reconnect the sampling line if needed. Make sure the connector is not loose, cracked, contaminated, or partially inserted. Expected outcome: The line connects firmly and the monitor begins sampling normally. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service after confirming stable CO2 operation. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Full Length of the Sampling Line
steps.instructions: Follow the sampling line from the monitor to the patient connection. Look for: Kinks; Pinched tubing; Crushed sections; Fluid contamination; Loose fittings; Cracks or splits; Disconnected patient end; Tubing trapped under equipment, bed rails, or bedding. Expected outcome: The sampling path is open, connected, and free of obvious damage. Why it matters: A kinked or blocked sampling line can stop the monitor from pulling an adequate CO2 sample.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Line
steps.instructions: Install a new compatible sampling line. Do not try to reuse a line that is wet, visibly contaminated, kinked, cracked, or suspected of being blocked. Expected outcome: CO2 sampling resumes with a stable waveform and EtCO2 value. If replacing the sampling line resolves the issue, document the defective or blocked disposable and return the monitor to service. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Secretions in the Line
steps.instructions: Inspect the removed sampling line for condensation, fluid, secretions, or visible blockage. If moisture or contamination is present, discard the line and use a new compatible line. Expected outcome: The replacement line remains dry and sampling is restored. Why it matters: Moisture or secretions can block the sampling path and cause flow-related alarms or inaccurate CO2 readings.

steps.title: Check the Patient Connection or Airway Adapter
steps.instructions: Inspect the cannula, airway adapter, or sample port connection depending on how the NM3 is being used. Confirm that the patient end is properly connected, not obstructed, and not installed in a way that blocks sample flow. Expected outcome: The sampling line has a clear path from the patient source to the monitor. If the patient connection was loose or incorrect and correcting it restores normal CO2 readings, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Leaks at Fittings and Adapters
steps.instructions: Inspect all external CO2 sampling connections. Gently move the line and fittings while watching the waveform and EtCO2 value. Look for readings that drop out when the line is moved. Expected outcome: CO2 readings remain stable when the tubing and connections are gently moved. Why it matters: Small leaks can cause weak, unstable, or missing CO2 readings even when the line appears connected.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Is Pulling Sample Flow
steps.instructions: With a known-good sampling line installed, listen for normal sampling pump activity if applicable and observe whether the monitor detects the line and begins sampling. Do not open the monitor or attempt internal pump repair during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The NM3 recognizes the line and produces a stable waveform when connected to an appropriate CO2 source.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: If available, compare the suspected monitor using: A new sampling line; A known-good airway adapter or cannula; A known-good patient simulator or approved CO2 test source; Another monitor setup if available. Expected outcome: The issue follows the disposable accessory or external setup, not the monitor. If the NM3 works normally with known-good accessories, replace the faulty disposable or external accessory and return the monitor to service. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Failure With Multiple New Lines
steps.instructions: If the same blocked, leak, kink, or disconnected condition appears with multiple new compatible sampling lines, suspect a monitor-side issue such as inlet damage, sensor module fault, flow restriction, or internal sampling pump problem. Expected outcome: External causes are ruled out before removing the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Philips NM3 was alarming for a blocked or disconnected sampling line and was not displaying a reliable CO2 waveform.
CCR.Cause: Found the CO2 sampling line kinked near the patient connection, preventing proper sample flow to the monitor.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the sampling line, verified secure connection at the NM3, confirmed stable EtCO2 waveform, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred during active patient monitoring
Whether EtCO2 value was missing, low, or unstable
Whether the CO2 waveform was absent or intermittent

title: Philips NM3 Capnography Monitor - SPo₂ Sensor/Cable Not Detected or Unreliable Reading
description: Troubleshooting missing, intermittent, or unreliable SpO₂ readings caused by sensor, cable, connection, placement, or monitor issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Philips NM3 is actively monitoring a patient and SpO₂ is required, do not continue relying on an unreliable reading. Move SpO₂ monitoring to another approved monitor or confirm clinical staff have alternate monitoring in place before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely monitored. Why it matters: An unreliable SpO₂ reading can delay recognition of oxygen desaturation or poor perfusion.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is no SpO₂ sensor detected, no SpO₂ value displayed, intermittent SpO₂ reading, erratic or unstable SpO₂ value, pulse rate missing or inconsistent, or poor pleth waveform. Expected outcome: The exact failure behavior is confirmed. If the issue was a misunderstanding or temporary disconnect, correct it and stop.

steps.title: Check the SpO₂ Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the sensor connection at the patient end and at the NM3 module/monitor connection point. Make sure the connector is fully seated, aligned correctly, and not loose. Expected outcome: The sensor is firmly connected and recognized by the monitor. If reconnecting the sensor restores a stable SpO₂ reading, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO₂ Sensor for Damage
steps.instructions: Look for cracked housings, bent pins, torn cable insulation, exposed wires, fluid contamination, crushed sections, or a damaged clip/adhesive sensor. Expected outcome: The sensor is physically intact. If the sensor is damaged, replace it with a known-good compatible sensor and stop if readings return.

steps.title: Confirm Sensor Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify that the SpO₂ sensor and any extension cable are compatible with the Philips NM3 configuration being used. Avoid mixing unknown, damaged, expired, or questionable third-party accessories. Expected outcome: A compatible sensor/cable is being used. Why it matters: Incompatible sensors may not be detected or may produce unreliable values.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good SpO₂ Sensor
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good SpO₂ sensor that has already worked on another compatible monitor. Expected outcome: The NM3 detects the sensor and displays a stable SpO₂ value. If the known-good sensor works, the original sensor is the likely cause. Replace the sensor and stop.

steps.title: Check the SpO₂ Extension Cable, If Used
steps.instructions: If an extension cable is installed, remove it and connect the sensor directly if possible. If direct connection works, test with a known-good extension cable. Expected outcome: The reading remains stable with a good cable path. If bypassing or replacing the extension cable resolves the issue, replace the faulty cable and stop.

steps.title: Check Patient Placement and Signal Quality
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor is placed correctly and that the site is appropriate for SpO₂ monitoring. Common causes of poor readings include motion, cold extremities, poor perfusion, nail polish, excessive ambient light, poor sensor alignment, or a sensor that is too loose or too tight. Expected outcome: A proper sensor site produces a stable pleth waveform and SpO₂ value. Why it matters: A bad patient signal can look like an equipment failure.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitor
steps.instructions: If safe and appropriate, compare the sensor and patient reading against another working SpO₂ monitor. Expected outcome: The reading is reasonably consistent between devices. If the alternate monitor reads correctly using the same patient site and accessory setup, suspect the NM3 or its SpO₂ input path.

steps.title: Inspect the NM3 SpO₂ Connector Port
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the SpO₂ input connector for bent pins, debris, fluid residue, looseness, corrosion, or signs of impact damage. Do not force connectors or attempt internal repair during floor troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The connector is clean, secure, and undamaged. If the connector is damaged or loose, remove the unit from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor/Module if Safe
steps.instructions: If the NM3 is not in active patient use, power cycle the monitor/module and reconnect the SpO₂ sensor. Expected outcome: The monitor restarts normally and detects the SpO₂ sensor. If the issue clears after restart, document the behavior and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Failure Across Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Test using known-good compatible sensors and cables. If the NM3 still does not detect SpO₂ or produces unreliable readings with verified accessories, the problem is no longer likely to be external. Expected outcome: External accessory causes are ruled out. If failure persists, remove the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Philips NM3 was not detecting the SpO₂ sensor and displayed no reliable SpO₂ value.
CCR.Cause: Found damaged SpO₂ extension cable causing intermittent sensor detection during movement.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the SpO₂ extension cable with a known-good compatible cable, verified stable SpO₂ and pulse rate reading, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue was no detection, intermittent detection, or unreliable reading
Power behavior or whether the issue cleared after restart
Environmental factors such as motion, cold extremities, poor perfusion, nail polish, or excessive ambient light
Any indicator lights, alarms, or displayed messages
Any visible damage, fluid residue, bent pins, or loose connector

title: Philips NM3 Capnography Monitor - Alarm Limits, Patient Type, or Setup Mismatch Causing Nuisance Alarms
description: Troubleshooting nuisance alarms caused by alarm limits, patient type, monitoring setup, or configuration mismatch.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is stable and being properly monitored before changing any alarm settings or setup selections. Expected outcome: Patient condition is verified before assuming the alarm is false. Why it matters: Alarm limits exist for patient safety. A nuisance alarm may still be warning about a real clinical condition. If the alarm reflects a true patient condition, stop troubleshooting and notify clinical staff.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what alarm is occurring, how often it occurs, and whether it happens with one patient, one room, one monitor, or multiple setups. Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to alarm settings, patient type, accessory setup, or repeated false alarm behavior. Why it matters: Nuisance alarms are often caused by setup mismatch rather than device failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Patient Type or Patient Category
steps.instructions: Check whether the monitor or host system is set for the correct patient type, such as adult, pediatric, or neonatal, if applicable. Expected outcome: The selected patient type matches the patient being monitored. Why it matters: Patient type can affect default alarm limits, parameter behavior, and expected respiratory values. If correcting the patient type resolves the nuisance alarm, document the setup correction and stop.

steps.title: Review Alarm Limits With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions: Check the alarm limits for EtCO2, respiration rate, FiCO2, apnea/no-breath time, or any other active CO2-related alarm. Do not independently choose clinical alarm limits. Confirm with the responsible clinical staff that the limits are appropriate for the patient and care area. Expected outcome: Alarm limits are appropriate for the patient condition and clinical environment. Why it matters: Limits set too tight can cause repeated nuisance alarms. Limits set too wide can delay recognition of patient deterioration. If alarm limit adjustment by clinical staff resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check Whether Defaults Were Changed or Not Restored
steps.instructions: Determine whether the monitor was recently used in another department, on another patient population, or for a different care situation. Expected outcome: Any unusual alarm limit, patient type, or setup configuration is identified. Why it matters: A monitor moved between areas may retain settings that are not appropriate for the next patient use.

steps.title: Inspect the CO2 Sampling Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the correct sampling line, airway adapter, cannula, water trap, and connection path are being used for the monitoring situation. Expected outcome: The sampling setup matches the intended patient application and is fully connected. Why it matters: Incorrect or partially connected accessories can cause erratic CO2 readings, poor waveforms, and unnecessary alarms. If correcting the accessory setup resolves the nuisance alarms, document the accessory or setup issue and stop.

steps.title: Check for Sampling Line Kinks, Moisture, or Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the sampling line for kinks, pinching, liquid contamination, blockage, or poor routing near the patient or bed. Expected outcome: The line is clear, dry, routed safely, and not pulling on the patient connection. Why it matters: Restricted sampling flow can cause unstable EtCO2 readings, delayed breath detection, or false no-breath alarms.

steps.title: Evaluate the CO2 Waveform and Numeric Values
steps.instructions: Observe whether the waveform is stable, intermittent, weak, distorted, or missing. Compare alarm behavior to the displayed EtCO2 and respiration rate values. Expected outcome: The displayed alarm matches the waveform and numeric trend. Why it matters: A clean waveform with inappropriate alarm limits points toward setup/configuration. A poor waveform points toward accessory, sampling, or patient-interface issues.

steps.title: Check for Patient Movement or Poor Cannula Positioning
steps.instructions: If used with a nasal/oral cannula, confirm it is positioned correctly and not displaced by talking, movement, blankets, masks, or oxygen delivery equipment. Expected outcome: The cannula remains properly positioned during normal patient movement. Why it matters: Poor cannula placement can cause intermittent breath detection and nuisance respiratory alarms.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Is Connected to the Correct Host or Display System
steps.instructions: If the Philips NM3 is being used with a host monitor or central station, confirm the module is seated properly and communicating correctly. Expected outcome: The CO2 module data displays consistently without intermittent dropouts. Why it matters: Communication or module seating issues can appear as nuisance alarms or intermittent parameter loss.

steps.title: Compare With a Known Good Setup
steps.instructions: If available, test the NM3 with a known good sampling line, known good host connection, and appropriate test setup or simulator. Expected outcome: The module performs normally with known good external accessories and setup. Why it matters: This helps separate a setup/accessory issue from a possible module fault.

steps.title: Check for Repeatability
steps.instructions: Determine whether the same nuisance alarms follow the NM3 module, follow the patient room, follow the sampling accessories, or occur only with one clinical setup. Expected outcome: The source of the issue is isolated. Why it matters: A problem that follows the module may need bench evaluation. A problem that follows the setup or room is less likely to be internal device failure.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported repeated nuisance CO2 alarms on a Philips NM3 capnography monitor during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Patient type and alarm limits were reviewed, and the monitor was found set up with limits not appropriate for the current patient category.
CCR.Resolution: Correct patient setup was confirmed with clinical staff, alarm limits were adjusted by clinical staff, CO2 waveform was verified stable, and the monitor was returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether limits were changed by clinical staff
Whether issue followed the module, room, patient, or accessories

title: Philips NM3 Capnography Monitor - Power, Battery, Display, or Startup Failure
description: Troubleshooting NM3 power, battery, display, or startup issues caused by external power, accessories, settings, or device failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Philips NM3 is being used for active patient monitoring, do not continue using it if the screen is blank, the unit will not power on, or the device cannot complete startup. Move the patient to another working monitor or approved monitoring method before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient CO2 monitoring continues safely on another device. Why it matters: A capnography monitor that cannot power on, display values, or complete startup cannot be trusted for ventilation monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom before replacing parts or sending the unit out. Check whether the issue is: No power at all; Powers on briefly, then shuts down; Display is blank but power indicators are active; Display is dim, distorted, or frozen; Startup screen appears but does not complete; Battery does not hold charge; Device only works when plugged in; Device only fails on battery power. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified. Why it matters: A dead unit, weak battery, display issue, and startup lockup are different problems with different likely causes.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug the NM3 into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. If possible, test the outlet with another device or outlet tester per facility practice. Avoid using a questionable power strip, loose wall outlet, or non-approved extension cord. Expected outcome: The device receives stable AC power. If this resolves the issue: Document the faulty outlet or power source and return the NM3 to service only after functional checks pass.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check the power cord and external power supply, if applicable, for: Loose connection; Bent or damaged plug; Cut or crushed cable; Damaged strain relief; Burn marks; Excessive heat; Missing hospital-grade plug integrity. Reseat all power connections firmly. Expected outcome: The NM3 powers on normally with a secure power connection. If this resolves the issue: Replace the damaged or loose power accessory and document the correction.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Power Supply or Cord
steps.instructions: If your facility has another compatible NM3 power supply or approved power cord, swap it for testing. Do not use an incorrect voltage, incorrect polarity, or non-approved adapter. Expected outcome: The unit powers on normally with a known-good power source. Why it matters: External power accessories are easier and safer to verify before suspecting an internal power fault. If this resolves the issue: Remove the failed power accessory from service and replace it.

steps.title: Check for Battery-Only Failure
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and observe whether the NM3 remains powered on. Then reconnect AC power and confirm whether charging indicators or battery status messages appear. Look for signs such as: Battery low message; Battery not charging; Unit shuts off immediately when unplugged; Battery icon missing or abnormal; Unit only works on AC power. Expected outcome: Battery operation and charging behavior are understood. Why it matters: A device that works on AC but fails on battery likely has a battery, charging, or battery connection issue.

steps.title: Allow the Battery to Charge
steps.instructions: If the battery is fully discharged, connect the NM3 to AC power and allow charging per facility practice before retesting battery operation. Do not assume battery failure immediately after a long storage period. Expected outcome: The monitor powers up and operates after charging. If this resolves the issue: Document that the battery was depleted and the device passed power-up and battery verification after charging.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Area Externally
steps.instructions: If the battery is accessible without deep disassembly, inspect for: Loose battery door; Battery not seated correctly; Swollen battery; Corrosion; Cracked housing; Liquid intrusion; Heat or odor. Do not continue troubleshooting if the battery appears swollen, leaking, overheated, or chemically damaged. Expected outcome: Battery condition is safe enough for continued evaluation. Why it matters: Battery damage can create safety risks and should move the device directly to repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check the Display Brightness and Screen Visibility
steps.instructions: If the unit appears powered but the display is blank or hard to see, check whether the display is dimmed, in standby, or affected by viewing angle or room lighting. Look for: Power LED; Backlight glow; Audible startup tones; Button response; Alarm sounds; CO2 pump or internal fan noise, if present. Expected outcome: You determine whether this is a true no-power issue or a display/backlight issue. If this resolves the issue: Correct the display setting and verify normal operation.

steps.title: Check for Frozen Startup or Software Lockup
steps.instructions: If the Philips NM3 powers on but freezes during startup, disconnect patient accessories, sampling line, and external connections. Power the device off, wait briefly, then restart it on known-good AC power. Expected outcome: The monitor completes startup without external accessories connected. Why it matters: A connected accessory, communication cable, or transient fault may interfere with startup. If this resolves the issue: Reconnect accessories one at a time to identify the item causing the startup problem.

steps.title: Check CO2 Sampling Line and Accessories
steps.instructions: Remove the sampling line or airway adapter from the monitor and inspect for blockage, liquid contamination, broken connectors, or incorrect accessory type. Then restart the monitor without the accessory connected. Expected outcome: The NM3 starts normally without a defective or contaminated accessory attached. Why it matters: Some monitor faults may appear during startup or self-test when an accessory problem is present.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Fluid Intrusion
steps.instructions: Inspect the NM3 housing, display, buttons, ports, and connector areas. Look for: Cracked case; Damaged display lens; Sticky buttons; Broken power jack; Bent pins; Evidence of cleaning fluid intrusion; Dried residue near seams or connectors; Burn smell or unusual heat. Expected outcome: Any visible damage or contamination is identified. Why it matters: Damage or fluid intrusion can cause unsafe power, display, and startup behavior.

steps.title: Test with Minimum Configuration
steps.instructions: Power the NM3 using known-good AC power with only the minimum required accessories connected. Do not connect the device to a patient for this test. Confirm: Unit powers on; Display is readable; Startup completes; No critical startup error remains; Battery or charging status appears normal; Controls respond normally. Expected outcome: The device completes a clean startup in a controlled bench condition. If this resolves the issue: Add accessories back one at a time and document the failed accessory or condition found.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: After the unit powers up, perform a basic operational check appropriate for your facility before returning it to service. Verify: Display remains stable; Buttons respond; Alarm audio works; CO2 function initializes; Battery status is acceptable; No startup or system errors remain; Device remains powered during brief movement of the power cord and connector. Expected outcome: The NM3 operates normally and is safe for clinical use. If this resolves the issue: Return the device to service after documentation.

steps.title: Do Not Ignore Intermittent Power Failures
steps.instructions: If the device powers off when moved, loses display intermittently, resets randomly, or only works when the cord is held in a certain position, remove it from service. Expected outcome: Intermittent power problems are treated as repair issues, not cleared as “could not duplicate” without adequate evaluation. Why it matters: Intermittent power loss during patient monitoring can create a serious patient safety risk.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Philips NM3 would not power on and the display remained blank when connected for patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Verified the wall outlet was functional, but the NM3 power supply cable had an intermittent connection at the monitor input.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the faulty power supply, confirmed normal startup, verified display operation, checked charging status, and returned the NM3 to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or startup messages observed
Accessories swapped or removed during testing
Power behavior on AC power and battery power
Environmental factors such as outlet condition, recent power loss, cleaning fluid exposure, heat, odor, or fluid intrusion
Indicator lights, battery icon, charge status, display condition, startup behavior, and button response
Final device status: returned to service, accessory replaced, labeled Out of Service, or sent for repair

title: Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 Radiographic System - SkyPlate wireless detector not connecting, charging, or acquiring images
description: Troubleshooting SkyPlate battery, detector connection, readiness, or image acquisition failures before determining that service evaluation is required.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the room from clinical use if the SkyPlate detector cannot reliably connect, indicate readiness, or acquire an image. Do not continue patient imaging with an unreliable wireless detector. Notify Radiology staff and redirect examinations to another functioning radiographic system as clinically appropriate. Expected outcome: No patient exam is attempted using a detector with unresolved connection, charging, or acquisition concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Detector Complaint
steps.instructions: With the system out of patient use, obtain the exact report and reproduce the condition without making an exposure. Determine whether the problem is: SkyPlate not detected by the system; detector appears connected but does not become ready; battery will not charge or quickly loses charge; detector disconnects intermittently; exposure is prevented because the detector is not ready; or exposure occurs during controlled testing, but no image appears. Review and document any message, detector icon, battery indication, readiness status, or workstation error. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to power, charging, connection, readiness, or post-acquisition image transfer. Why it matters: Philips describes the SkyPlate as communicating directly with the X-ray system in real time, with image data transferred to the Eleva workspot after acquisition.

steps.title: Inspect the SkyPlate for External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the detector before further testing. Check for: cracked housing, damaged corners, swelling, or signs of impact; fluid intrusion or contamination; damaged battery compartment, battery contacts, or connector area; bent, loose, or damaged accessories; signs that the detector was dropped, crushed, or improperly handled. Do not use a visibly damaged detector for patient imaging or continue charging a battery that is swollen, excessively hot, leaking, or damaged. Expected outcome: The detector and battery are either externally intact for continued evaluation or removed from service due to physical damage.

steps.title: Check the Detector Battery and Charging Equipment
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the approved SkyPlate battery, inspecting it for damage or abnormal heat. Place the battery in the dedicated charger and observe charging indications. If another known-working, compatible SkyPlate battery is available, test the detector with that battery. Likewise, test the suspect battery in a known-working compatible charger when appropriate. Philips documentation describes SkyPlate configurations with exchangeable batteries and a dedicated battery charger; charging failure may involve the battery, charger, charging contacts, or detector power path. Expected outcome: A charged known-working battery powers the detector normally, identifying a likely battery or charger issue; or the detector remains unavailable with a known-working charged battery, indicating that troubleshooting should continue. If replacing or properly charging the battery resolves the issue, verify detector readiness and controlled image acquisition, document the repair, and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Detector Is Assigned or Attached to the C90 System
steps.instructions: Verify that the SkyPlate being used is intended for and configured with the affected DigitalDiagnost C90 system. If the department shares SkyPlates between compatible Philips rooms, confirm that the detector has been properly attached or associated with this specific room according to the site’s approved workflow. Philips states that shared SkyPlates must be attached to a specific system before use and that one SkyPlate at a time is recognized by each system. Check for: wrong detector selected or brought into the room; detector recently moved from another compatible Philips system; system waiting for a different assigned detector; detector identification mismatch or failure to recognize the installed SkyPlate. Expected outcome: The correct detector is recognized by the C90 system and displays an available or ready state. If correctly attaching the detector resolves the issue, complete a safe functional verification, document the action, and stop.

steps.title: Evaluate Wireless Connection Conditions
steps.instructions: Place the SkyPlate near the system in its normal operating area and observe whether the connection becomes stable. Check for: connection loss only when the detector is moved farther from the room system; detector placed behind excessive shielding, inside a storage location, or in an unusual position during testing; intermittent connection when moving or repositioning the detector; recent room changes, added shielding, or equipment changes near the detector communication area. The SkyPlate uses a dedicated point-to-point wireless connection with the Philips X-ray system that is separate from the hospital IT network; a general PACS or hospital Wi-Fi outage should not by itself explain failure of the detector to connect to the C90 acquisition system. Expected outcome: The detector maintains a stable connection in its intended operating location, or an ongoing wireless/system communication issue is identified.

steps.title: Check for Approved Wired Backup Operation, If Equipped
steps.instructions: Determine whether the system has the Philips-approved SkyPlate backup cable available for this detector configuration. Inspect the cable for damage, bent connectors, contamination, or loose connections. If allowed by facility procedure and supported by the installed configuration, connect the approved backup cable and determine whether the detector is recognized. Philips documentation identifies backup cable support for SkyPlate operation and image readout when wireless communication is unavailable. Expected outcome: Detector operation returns with the approved cable connected, supporting a wireless communication problem; or the detector remains unavailable, suggesting a detector, battery, system interface, or acquisition-chain failure. If wired backup restores operation, do not assume the wireless issue is corrected. Label the wireless concern for follow-up evaluation unless the system’s approved workflow specifically permits continued wired use.

steps.title: Review Workstation and Acquisition Readiness Status
steps.instructions: At the Eleva workspot, verify that the system is properly started, the correct room configuration is active, and no detector-related, acquisition-related, or system fault message remains displayed. Observe whether: the detector is shown as available; battery status is recognized; the system reaches ready-for-exposure status; a detector fault or communication fault remains present; the workstation requires an approved restart after a transient communication interruption. Perform only a normal operator-level system restart when safe, after documenting displayed messages and ensuring the system is not in clinical use. Expected outcome: The workspot recognizes the SkyPlate and permits normal detector readiness, or the fault remains reproducible.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Verification
steps.instructions: Only after the detector is connected, charged, recognized, and ready, perform an authorized test exposure using the facility’s approved radiographic quality-control method or appropriate test object. Do not use a patient for troubleshooting or verification. Verify that: the detector reports ready before exposure; the exposure completes without detector fault; the image appears at the Eleva workspot; no repeat exposure is required due to missing image transfer; image display appears normal for the test performed. Philips states that acquired SkyPlate images are stored in detector memory until securely transferred to the Eleva workspot, and the system synchronizes detector readiness with X-ray generation. Expected outcome: A complete test acquisition confirms normal detector connection, readiness, and image transfer. If the detector successfully acquires and displays a test image without recurrence, return the system to service according to department procedure and document the result.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the DigitalDiagnost C90 SkyPlate wireless detector would not connect and the room could not acquire images.
CCR.Cause: Verified that the SkyPlate remained unavailable with a charged known-working battery and could not establish normal detector readiness at the Eleva workspot.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the DigitalDiagnost C90 room from service, labeled it Out of Service, and escalated the detector communication failure for Philips/bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact detector or workstation message displayed.
Detector housing and battery inspected for damage.
Detector recently moved from another Philips room.
Detector recognized or not recognized by the C90 system.
Approved backup cable tested, if equipped.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.

title: Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 Radiographic System - Ceiling Suspension Movement, Tracking, or Auto-Positioning Failure
description: Troubleshooting ceiling-suspended tube movement, tracking mismatch, or failed automatic positioning caused by obstructions, setup, control, or mechanical concerns.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the room from clinical use before testing any ceiling suspension movement, tracking, or automatic positioning function. If a patient is positioned for an examination, have clinical staff move the patient away from the affected system or use another approved radiographic room before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is not exposed to unintended equipment motion, delayed imaging, collision risk, or repeat positioning attempts during evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Failure Reported
steps.instructions: With the system out of patient use, obtain the complaint from staff and identify the specific symptom: Ceiling suspension will not move in one or more directions. Tube head moves manually but does not track with the detector. Automatic positioning will not start. Automatic positioning starts but stops before reaching position. Tube and detector appear misaligned after movement. Movement is jerky, noisy, drifting, or unexpectedly restricted. A movement, tracking, or positioning message appears at the workstation or tube head. Record whether the failure occurred during table, wall stand, or free-exposure positioning and whether the issue is intermittent or repeatable. Expected outcome: The affected motion function and operating condition are clearly identified before testing.

steps.title: Inspect the Travel Path and Room Environment
steps.instructions: Inspect the ceiling suspension travel path, tube head, detector stand or table area, rails, cable routing, ceiling covers, and nearby equipment for visible interference. Look for: IV poles, stools, portable equipment, ceiling-mounted accessories, or other objects within the movement path. Hanging cables or accessories that may snag during movement. Damaged, loose, shifted, or contacting ceiling-rail covers. Obvious physical interference between the suspension and surrounding structures. Evidence that the tube head, collimator, detector assembly, or suspension was struck. Do not continue motorized movement testing if the suspension binds, contacts the structure, produces abnormal grinding noises, or appears physically unstable. Expected outcome: The travel path is clear and no visible damage or collision condition is present. If an obstruction is removed and normal movement returns, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Verify System Power and Ready Status
steps.instructions: Confirm that the radiographic system is powered on normally and has completed startup without active system fault messages. Check the Eleva workstation and tube head display for: System not ready messages. Movement or positioning warnings. Communication faults. Emergency-stop or inhibited-motion indications. Any fault that appeared when the reported movement failure occurred. Where present, verify that emergency-stop devices or room safety controls are not engaged. Do not bypass any safety control. Expected outcome: The system is in an operational state that permits movement testing. If a clearly identified engaged safety control is safely reset according to facility practice and motion returns normally, document and stop.

steps.title: Check the Tube Head Movement Control and Brake Release
steps.instructions: Test manual positioning only with the room clear and no patient present. Use the normal tube head movement controls and brake-release function to determine whether the ceiling suspension releases and travels smoothly in the permitted axes. DigitalDiagnost C90 specifications identify tube head controls with a capacitive sensor for three-axis brake release. Observe for: Brake release not responding. One axis remaining locked while others move. Excessive resistance or drifting. Movement that stops unexpectedly. Abnormal noise or vibration. Expected outcome: Manual movement releases and travels smoothly without binding, uncontrolled drift, or noise. If manual movement is abnormal, stop further auto-position testing and escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: Verify the Selected Examination Setup and Positioning Function
steps.instructions: Confirm that the correct examination type, detector location, and positioning workflow are selected for the attempted movement. Where the installed configuration supports automatic positioning, verify that the requested position is appropriate for the selected table or wall-stand workflow. A mismatch between the selected examination geometry and actual detector location may prevent expected tracking or automatic positioning behavior. Expected outcome: The selected workflow matches the intended detector and exam position. If correcting the selection restores proper motion, document the setup correction and stop.

steps.title: Test Tracking Function Without Exposure
steps.instructions: With the travel path clear and the system out of clinical use, activate the applicable tracking function and perform a controlled positioning movement. Observe whether: The ceiling suspension follows the intended detector movement. The tube head remains appropriately aligned to the receptor. Tracking begins and completes without stopping. The position display or system status changes appropriately. An error message appears during tracking. Do not perform exposures simply to evaluate a mechanical movement complaint. Expected outcome: The tube and detector move together as intended for the selected configuration. If tracking repeatedly fails, becomes misaligned, or generates a fault, remove the unit from service and escalate.

steps.title: Test Automatic Positioning, If Equipped
steps.instructions: If the system configuration includes automatic positioning, select a known routine examination position and command movement only after confirming the room is unoccupied and the motion path is clear. Observe whether the system: Accepts the positioning command. Begins movement normally. Completes travel to the selected position. Stops unexpectedly before reaching the intended location. Moves the detector and tube in unison where applicable. Produces an error, unusual noise, or collision concern. Philips describes supported configurations as allowing movement to a selected exam position and, with the optional fully automated ceiling suspension, coordinated movement of the detector and tube. Expected outcome: Automatic positioning completes smoothly and consistently. If movement stops, misaligns, or behaves unpredictably, discontinue testing and escalate.

steps.title: Check for Repeatability and Document Visible Findings
steps.instructions: If movement returns after a simple external correction, repeat the same non-exposure positioning action once or twice to confirm that the problem does not immediately recur. Document: Direction or axis affected. Table or wall-stand workflow involved. Error messages displayed. Whether manual movement works. Whether tracking or automatic positioning works. Any obstruction removed or setup correction made. Any abnormal noise, binding, collision mark, shifted cover, or suspected structural concern. Expected outcome: The resolved issue is repeatably corrected, or the failure is clearly documented for service escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the DigitalDiagnost C90 ceiling suspension would not follow the wall stand during positioning and automatic positioning stopped before reaching the selected chest position.
CCR.Cause: Verified clear travel path and correct wall-stand exam selection; manual suspension movement was possible, but tracking repeatedly stopped with tube and detector alignment incomplete.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the DigitalDiagnost C90 from service, applied an Out of Service label, documented the repeatable tracking failure, and referred the unit for qualified Philips/service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Room configuration involved: table, wall stand, or free exposure
Ceiling rail, cover, cable, or tube head damage noted
Abnormal noise, vibration, binding, drift, or collision concern
System restart outcome, if performed according to facility procedure

title: Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 Radiographic System - X-Ray Generator Communication or Exposure Failure
description: Troubleshooting generator communication faults, failed exposure attempts, no X-ray output, or exposure-control problems before escalating for internal repair.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient and Radiation Safety First
steps.instructions:
Remove the room from clinical use until reliable exposure operation is confirmed. If a patient is present, have clinical staff move the patient to another appropriate radiographic system before troubleshooting.

Do not perform test exposures with a patient present. Any radiation-producing test must follow facility radiation-safety policy and be performed only by qualified personnel.

Expected outcome: No patient depends on a radiographic system with uncertain exposure capability.

Why it matters: The DigitalDiagnost C90 is designed for routine radiographic examinations, and an unresolved exposure-control or generator communication failure can prevent imaging or create an unsafe operating condition.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Exact Failure Condition
steps.instructions:
Obtain the original complaint and observe the system startup and attempted workflow while the unit is out of patient service.

Document whether the problem is:

Generator communication error displayed at the workspot.
Exposure preparation begins but exposure does not occur.
Exposure hand switch does not initiate preparation or exposure.
System appears ready, but no image is acquired.
Failure is intermittent or occurs only with a specific detector, table, wall stand, or exam setup.
A specific error code or message is present.

Photograph or record the exact error message before restarting the system.

Expected outcome: The reported issue is clearly identified and repeatable, or the intermittent condition is documented for escalation.

steps.title: 3. Verify Room Power and System Startup
steps.instructions:
Confirm that the radiographic room has normal facility power and that all user-accessible system components complete startup normally.

Inspect for:

Dark or unresponsive acquisition workspot.
Abnormal startup messages.
Generator, detector, tube head, or room component not recognized.
UPS, breaker, or room power indication that suggests an external supply problem.

Do not open generator cabinets or reset internal breakers unless specifically authorized under your service procedures.

Expected outcome: The acquisition workspot and associated room components start normally without power-related indications.

If power restoration or a permitted reset resolves the issue: Verify safe system operation according to facility policy, document the result, and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 4. Check the Acquisition Workspot and Generator Communication Indications
steps.instructions:
Verify that the Eleva Workspot, which incorporates generator control, is responsive and displays the expected system-ready condition. The DigitalDiagnost C90 configuration includes an Eleva Workspot with integrated generator control and a hand switch for exposure release.

Check for:

Frozen or unresponsive user interface.
Generator shown as unavailable, disconnected, or not ready.
Repeating communication warning after startup.
Exam setup unable to progress to ready status.

Perform only the normal operator-accessible restart procedure permitted by facility practice. Do not bypass communication faults or attempt exposure while a generator warning remains active.

Expected outcome: The workspot recognizes the generator and allows the system to reach a normal ready condition.

If communication is restored and remains stable: Complete required operational verification and return the system only if it passes all safety requirements.

steps.title: 5. Inspect Accessible Exposure-Control Connections
steps.instructions:
With the system safely powered down or placed in the appropriate safe state, visually inspect accessible connections associated with exposure operation.

Check:

Exposure hand switch cable and connector for damage, strain, looseness, bent pins, or contamination.
Accessible workstation and peripheral cables for obvious disconnection or damage.
Any user-accessible detector or room-component cable involved in the failed workflow.
Signs of fluid intrusion, crushed cable jackets, exposed conductors, or damaged connectors.

Do not disconnect internal generator wiring or remove covers.

Expected outcome: External cables and connectors are secure and free of visible damage.
