If a damaged accessory cable is identified: Remove the damaged accessory from service, replace it with an approved known-good equivalent when available, then retest according to facility policy.

steps.title: 6. Evaluate the Exposure Hand Switch
steps.instructions:
Inspect the hand switch for physical damage, sticking buttons, damaged strain relief, or intermittent operation.

Using a safe, non-patient workflow, determine whether:

The preparation stage responds when the hand switch is activated.
The exposure stage fails only when using that hand switch.
A known-good approved hand switch, when available and permitted, changes the behavior.

Expected outcome: The hand switch either functions normally or is identified as the likely external cause of the failed exposure request.

If replacement of a defective hand switch resolves the failure: Complete required exposure-performance verification before returning the room to service.

steps.title: 7. Confirm the Selected Exam and Detector Configuration
steps.instructions:
Verify that the selected examination, detector, and image receptor configuration are appropriate and recognized by the system.

Check for:

Detector not recognized or not ready.
Incorrect detector selected for the intended exposure.
Detector battery, wireless connection, backup cable, or docking condition preventing readiness, when applicable.
Failure occurring only at the table, wall stand, or free exposure configuration.

The DigitalDiagnost C90 may be configured with SkyPlate detectors and multiple room components; an exposure may be prevented when the intended acquisition path is not ready. Philips also identifies SkyPlate detector condition and backup cable checks as system maintenance items.

Expected outcome: The intended detector and exam pathway are properly recognized and ready for acquisition.

If the problem follows one detector or accessible detector cable: Remove that component from use and continue only with a verified functional configuration, when clinically and technically appropriate.

steps.title: 8. Inspect for Visible Conditions Preventing Exposure Readiness
steps.instructions:
Confirm that the system is not being prevented from reaching ready status by a visible external condition.

Check for:

Tube head, table, or wall stand not in the expected position for the selected exam.
Accessory, grid, detector, or bucky component not fully seated where applicable.
Active system warning requiring acknowledgement or correction.
Physical damage, collision evidence, unusual noise, overheating, odor, or fluid contamination.

Expected outcome: No visible mechanical, accessory, or readiness condition is preventing the requested exposure.

If any physical damage, unusual heat, odor, or evidence of collision is found: Stop immediately and keep the system out of service.

steps.title: 9. Perform a Controlled Non-Patient Exposure Verification Only When Authorized
steps.instructions:
Once external causes have been checked and the system reaches a normal ready condition, qualified personnel may perform the facility-approved non-patient exposure verification using appropriate radiation-safety controls.

Observe whether:

Preparation occurs normally.
Exposure is completed without generator communication error.
An image is successfully acquired.
The same fault immediately recurs.

Do not repeatedly expose in an attempt to clear an unresolved generator fault.

Expected outcome: A controlled verification exposure succeeds without repeating the fault.

If exposure succeeds and the system passes required quality and safety checks: Document the corrective action and return the system according to departmental procedure.

steps.title: 10. Review Preventive Maintenance Status and Recent Service History
steps.instructions:
Check whether the issue began after service, room power interruption, component replacement, detector change, software event, or intermittent communication complaint.

Verify whether generator and associated system checks are current. Philips lists generator functionality, cable connection, generator data backup, tube calibration, acquisition workspot functionality, and AWS remote connection checks within planned maintenance activities for the DigitalDiagnost C90.

Expected outcome: Relevant history is captured and overdue maintenance, recurring failures, or post-service patterns are identified.

If the exposure or communication failure remains unresolved: Stop troubleshooting and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 displayed a generator communication fault and would not complete an exposure from the Eleva Workspot.
CCR.Cause: Verified normal room power and detector recognition, but the exposure failure remained present after inspecting the accessible hand switch and external connections with no visible damage found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the radiographic room from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the displayed generator communication fault, and escalated the system for authorized Philips service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or code displayed
Whether failure occurred during preparation or exposure
Whether the exposure hand switch responded
Whether the detector was recognized and ready
Whether the problem occurred at table, wall stand, or free exposure setup
Accessible cables inspected or approved accessories swapped
Any unusual heat, sound, smell, damage, or collision history
Whether an authorized non-patient verification exposure was attempted

title: Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 Radiographic System - Collimator Light Field, Blade Movement, or Tube Alignment Issue
description: Troubleshooting incorrect light field, abnormal collimator blade movement, centering concerns, or suspected tube-to-detector alignment issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient and Radiation Safety First
steps.instructions:
Remove the radiographic room from clinical use until accurate collimation and alignment can be confirmed.

Do not troubleshoot positioning, collimation, or alignment concerns during a patient examination. If the concern occurred during use, direct staff to an alternate imaging room or approved backup workflow.

Expected outcome: No patient is exposed using equipment with unresolved collimation or alignment concerns.

Why it matters: An inaccurate light field or tube alignment concern may increase repeat imaging risk or create anatomy cutoff and unnecessary radiation exposure.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Exact Reported Problem
steps.instructions:
Obtain the complaint from imaging staff and reproduce the concern without a patient present. Determine whether the issue is:

Collimator light field does not illuminate.
Light field is dim, intermittent, distorted, or uneven.
Collimator blades will not open or close correctly.
Blade motion is slow, noisy, asymmetric, or stops unexpectedly.
The displayed or visible field does not appear centered to the detector or receptor.
The tube head appears physically rotated, angled, or not locking as expected.
The issue occurs only during a specific exam setup, receptor position, or automatic collimation workflow.

Record any displayed system message, unusual sound, recent impact, service activity, or room event.

Expected outcome: The failure is clearly identified before testing or escalation.

steps.title: 3. Visually Inspect the Tube Head and Collimator Exterior
steps.instructions:
Inspect the Eleva Tube Head and collimator assembly externally for:

Cracked, loose, shifted, or damaged covers.
Evidence of impact or collision.
Loose accessories or objects obstructing the collimator face.
Visible damage near the light field window.
Abnormal tube head sag, rotation, or failure to remain in the selected position.

Do not remove covers or attempt internal alignment adjustments.

Expected outcome: No external damage, obstruction, or obvious mechanical displacement is present.

If damage, impact evidence, or unstable positioning is found: Stop testing, remove the system from service, and escalate for repair.

steps.title: 4. Verify the System Is Fully Powered and Ready
steps.instructions:
Confirm that the system powers on normally and that the acquisition workstation and tube head interface are operational.

Check for:

Normal startup completion.
Active fault or warning messages.
Responsive tube head controls.
Normal room-ready status before attempting collimator operation.

The DigitalDiagnost C90 uses the Eleva Tube Head for in-room control, including collimation-related workflow functions.

Expected outcome: The system is fully operational without system faults that could explain unavailable or abnormal collimator control.

If a system fault is active: Document the message and address that fault before continuing collimator assessment.

steps.title: 5. Check Collimator Light Field Operation
steps.instructions:
With the unit out of patient use and positioned safely over the table or wall receptor area, activate the collimator light field using the normal user control.

Observe whether the field:

Turns on consistently.
Is bright enough to identify the illuminated area.
Appears rectangular and evenly defined.
Remains stable without flickering or dropping out.
Matches the approximate blade opening indicated by the controls.

Expected outcome: A clear, stable light field appears when activated.

If the light does not illuminate, flickers, is severely dim, or produces an irregular field: Stop clinical use and escalate for collimator evaluation.

steps.title: 6. Check for External Obstruction or Contamination at the Light Field Exit
steps.instructions:
Inspect the external collimator face and light exit area for removable contamination, tape residue, labels, accessory interference, or other obstruction that could distort the projected light field.

Clean only according to approved external cleaning practices. Do not open the collimator housing.

Expected outcome: The light field exit area is clean and unobstructed.

If cleaning or removal of an external obstruction restores a normal light field: Confirm normal operation through appropriate quality verification, document the correction, and return the system to service only after acceptance criteria are met.

steps.title: 7. Verify Collimator Blade Response Through Available Controls
steps.instructions:
Using the normal collimation controls at the tube head or workstation, command the field smaller and larger while observing the light field.

Check whether:

All field edges move when commanded.
The field opens and closes smoothly.
The blades stop at the selected field boundary.
Movement is symmetrical and repeatable.
Manual control responds if an automatic collimation workflow is suspected of contributing to the complaint.

Philips identifies the DigitalDiagnost C90 as using Eleva Tube Head control to support collimation workflow, and optional live-camera functionality may assist in viewing the collimated area.

Expected outcome: The visible light field changes smoothly and consistently with the selected collimation input.

If a blade does not move, moves unevenly, binds, chatters, or fails to repeat: Remove the system from service and escalate for authorized service evaluation.

steps.title: 8. Confirm the Issue Is Not Caused by Exam Setup or Detector Selection
steps.instructions:
Review whether the concern occurs with:

One specific exam protocol.
Table imaging only.
Wall stand imaging only.
One detector or receptor selection.
Automatic collimation behavior versus manual adjustment.
An incorrect active receptor or selected acquisition geometry.

Re-select the correct receptor and perform a non-patient setup check using an appropriate room configuration.

Expected outcome: The selected imaging receptor and geometry match the intended exam setup, and collimator behavior is consistent across applicable positions.

If the issue is isolated to one receptor position or configured workflow: Document that pattern and escalate with those details for more efficient service evaluation.

steps.title: 9. Check Tube Positioning, Rotation, and Locking Behavior
steps.instructions:
Move the tube head only through normal operator positioning functions and observe whether it:

Travels smoothly without abnormal drag or vibration.
Rotates into intended positions without drift.
Locks and remains stable when positioned.
Appears centered and square to the selected receptor when using normal detents or alignment positions.
Shows no external sign of collision-related displacement.

Do not attempt mechanical tube suspension adjustments or internal calibration.

Expected outcome: The tube head positions normally, remains stable, and appears properly oriented to the intended receptor.

If the tube head will not remain positioned, appears physically misaligned, or has abnormal movement: Remove the system from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: 10. Perform Authorized Collimation or Alignment Verification Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions:
If no obvious external cause is found and your Clinical Engineering department is authorized and equipped to perform radiographic quality-control checks, complete the approved light field-to-radiation field and centering verification procedure using proper radiation safety controls and test tools.

Do not use a patient for verification. Do not make collimator or tube alignment adjustments unless specifically trained, authorized, and working from approved service documentation.

Philips lists collimator functionality and fixation checks as annual planned-maintenance tasks and collimator component integrity checks every three years; planned maintenance is intended for qualified and authorized personnel.

Expected outcome: The light field, radiation field, and receptor alignment meet the facility’s approved quality-control acceptance criteria.

If verification fails or cannot be safely completed: Remove the system from service and escalate for authorized service evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Imaging staff reported that the DigitalDiagnost C90 collimator light field appeared off-center and the field blades did not close evenly during table exams.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no obstruction or visible damage, but the light field remained asymmetric and blade response was inconsistent during non-patient functional testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the radiographic room from service, labeled the system Out of Service, and escalated to authorized service for collimator and tube alignment evaluation before clinical reuse.

helpfulDetails: Exact reported symptom: no light, dim light, blade movement problem, or alignment concern.
Table, wall stand, or all imaging positions affected.
Specific protocol or receptor selection involved.
Any displayed fault or warning messages.
Tube head movement, locking, or drift observations.
Evidence of impact, collision, loose covers, or obstruction.
Whether authorized quality-control verification was performed.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.

title: Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 Radiographic System - Acquisition Workspot Boot, Connectivity, or Image-Transfer Failure
description: Troubleshooting Eleva workspot startup failure, loss of system communication, missing exams, or images not transferring to PACS.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the DigitalDiagnost C90 cannot reliably acquire, display, or transfer images, remove it from active patient imaging and notify Radiology staff. Do not expose a patient when the acquisition workspot is unavailable, patient/exam data cannot be confirmed, or image transfer status is uncertain. Move the exam to another functioning radiographic system when clinically necessary. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is performed only on equipment with reliable acquisition, review, and image-transfer capability.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Failure Condition
steps.instructions: With the room removed from patient use, determine the reported symptom: Eleva workspot does not power on; workspot begins booting but does not reach the application screen; system reaches the workspot but indicates disconnected components or unavailable acquisition; modality worklist does not load; images acquire locally but do not transfer to PACS; image-transfer queue shows failed, pending, or unavailable destination status; failure affects only one destination, such as PACS, while local image review remains available. Record displayed messages, icons, destination names, timestamps, and whether the failure started after a power interruption, network outage, system update, or room shutdown. Expected outcome: The issue is separated into boot, system communication, worklist/network, or image-transfer failure before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check Workspot Power and External Hardware
steps.instructions: Inspect the workspot, monitor, keyboard/mouse, connected power cables, surge protection or approved power distribution components, and any visible external interface equipment. Confirm: Workspot and monitor power cords are firmly seated; approved power source is available; no tripped breaker, disconnected emergency-power circuit, or room power interruption is reported; monitor power indicator is present; no damaged cord, loose connector, liquid intrusion, unusual heat, smell, or noise is present. The DigitalDiagnost C90 uses an Eleva workstation with solid-state drive-based operation for boot and access to examination information; a blank monitor or interrupted power path can appear to staff as a full acquisition failure. Expected outcome: The workspot powers normally and displays the Philips application startup or login screen. If resolved: Confirm normal startup and proceed only with approved functional verification before returning the room to service.

steps.title: Rule Out a Display-Only Problem
steps.instructions: If the workspot appears powered but the screen is blank or unreadable, verify the monitor power indicator, brightness controls if externally accessible, and securely seated external display connections. Observe whether there are signs the workstation itself is running, such as indicator lights, cooling fan activity, login audio, or response to permitted input actions. Expected outcome: A display issue is distinguished from a workstation boot failure. If the monitor remains blank while the workstation appears powered: Remove the system from service and escalate for bench or vendor evaluation rather than repeatedly cycling power.

steps.title: Perform One Controlled Restart When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient is involved, no active exposure is in progress, and there is no evidence of electrical damage or abnormal odor/heat, perform one controlled restart according to department-approved shutdown/startup practice. Do not perform repeated hard power cycles or force shutdowns during suspected image-transfer activity, because examination or transfer status may be unclear afterward. Observe and document: Whether the workspot reaches the normal application screen; any boot error, login issue, communication warning, or frozen screen; how far the startup progresses before failure. Expected outcome: The acquisition workspot either restores normal operation or presents a repeatable failure condition suitable for escalation. If resolved: Verify connectivity and image-transfer status before returning the system to clinical use.

steps.title: Verify Visible System Communication Status
steps.instructions: Once the workspot is operational, check for visible indications that required room components are available and communicating, such as generator, detector, tube head, table, or wall stand status as applicable to the installed room configuration. The C90 uses the Eleva workspot as part of the examination workflow and may coordinate system operation and image-related activity through the workstation interface. Check externally for: Loose visible network or communication cables at accessible equipment interfaces; recently disturbed room equipment, cable management, or workstation connections; a fault that appeared after moving equipment, cleaning, construction, power interruption, or network work; any component shown as offline, unavailable, or not ready. Do not open cabinets or reseat internal communication boards. Expected outcome: Required room components appear available and ready from the workspot. If a room component remains unavailable: Remove the room from service and escalate for system communication evaluation.

steps.title: Check Network Connectivity and Modality Worklist Function
steps.instructions: Determine whether the failure includes network-dependent functions: Modality worklist is unavailable or outdated; patient orders do not populate; PACS destination appears offline; other imaging rooms are also unable to access worklist or send images. Coordinate with Radiology/PACS or IT support as appropriate to determine whether there is a known network, RIS, modality worklist, or PACS outage. Inspect only accessible external network connections and documented room network indicators. Do not modify network settings, AE titles, IP configuration, DICOM destination settings, or security configuration without authorization. Philips identifies the DigitalDiagnost C90 as supporting integration into hospital IT infrastructure and PACS. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as either isolated to this radiographic room or associated with a broader network/PACS interruption. If a broader outage is confirmed: Document the system condition and coordinate restoration through the appropriate support pathway before clinical use resumes.

steps.title: Check Image-Transfer Queue and Local Image Availability
steps.instructions: If acquisition is possible but images are not arriving in PACS, confirm whether the images remain visible locally on the workspot and whether a transfer queue or failed-send status is displayed. Document: Exam accession or patient identifier according to privacy policy; time of acquisition; destination affected; whether images are pending, failed, or absent from the queue; whether a retry option is available to authorized users; whether the same failure occurs for more than one study. Do not repeat exposures solely because an image has not transferred unless Radiology has confirmed that the original image is unusable or unavailable and repeat imaging is clinically required. Expected outcome: Existing images are protected from unnecessary repeat exposure while the failure is isolated to local acquisition versus DICOM transfer. If an authorized resend successfully delivers the image: Confirm receipt with Radiology/PACS and document the restored transfer.

steps.title: Compare with Another Approved Destination or Room, When Available
steps.instructions: Coordinate with Radiology/PACS support to determine whether: Images from another modality are reaching the same PACS destination; the affected C90 can communicate with any approved configured destination; the problem is limited to one PACS destination, one worklist source, or the entire acquisition workspot. Do not create test patients, alter destinations, or transmit patient information outside approved workflows. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to the C90 workspot/system path or to an external PACS/network destination.

steps.title: Complete a Safe Functional Verification
steps.instructions: After any restored boot or connectivity condition, perform department-approved functional checks with no patient on the system. Confirm: Workspot completes startup normally; no active communication or system-ready warnings remain; modality worklist access is restored, when applicable; a permitted test or quality-control image workflow successfully processes and reaches the approved destination, when authorized; Radiology confirms expected image visibility before the room is returned to use. Expected outcome: The acquisition workspot and image-transfer workflow are reliable for clinical operation. If any failure repeats or verification cannot be completed: Keep the room out of service and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: Radiology reported that the Philips DigitalDiagnost C90 Eleva workspot powered on but would not send acquired images to PACS.
CCR.Cause: Workspot booted normally and images remained available locally, but the configured PACS destination showed failed transfer status after external connections and network availability were checked.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the room from clinical service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the affected exam-transfer status, and escalated to PACS/Philips support for image-transfer and system communication evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Workspot power indicator and monitor condition
Exact startup or communication message displayed
Whether the application reached the normal exam screen
Whether controlled restart was attempted and its result
Whether other imaging rooms were affected
Accessible cables or network connections inspected
Any recent power, IT, construction, or room-equipment changes
Presence of unusual heat, odor, or noise
Final device status and escalation pathway

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Critical Failure Detected. Service unit.
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips HeartStart MRx critical failure startup error that may affect defibrillation, monitoring, pacing, or clinical readiness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from clinical use immediately if the error appears during startup, patient care, transport, or readiness checks. Provide clinical staff with a known-good defibrillator before continuing troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and confirm the exact displayed message: Critical Failure Detected. Service unit. Document when the error appears and whether the device reaches normal operating screens.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Service if the Error Is Active
steps.instructions: If the error remains present, do not leave the MRx available for patient use. Attach an Out of Service label and notify the clinical area that the unit is unavailable.

steps.title: Check External Power First
steps.instructions: Connect the MRx to a known-good AC outlet using the proper power cord. Confirm the cord is fully seated and inspect for damage, loose plug fit, bent pins, or strain relief damage.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition and Seating
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the batteries. Inspect battery contacts and wells for debris, corrosion, damage, or contamination. Test with known-good charged MRx-compatible batteries if available.

steps.title: Check the Ready For Use Indicator
steps.instructions: Observe the Ready For Use indicator before and after powering the device on. Document ready status, red X or service indication, no indicator activity, or intermittent status change.

steps.title: Inspect the Unit for External Damage
steps.instructions: Check the case, display, therapy connector area, paddles or pads connector, ECG connector, printer door, battery wells, and power input area for damage or contamination.

steps.title: Disconnect Accessories and Recheck Startup
steps.instructions: Remove nonessential accessories and power the device on with only required power connected. Determine whether the error clears or remains.

steps.title: Reconnect Accessories One at a Time
steps.instructions: If the error clears after accessories are removed, reconnect one accessory at a time and restart the device as needed to isolate a faulty accessory.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check if the Unit Can Power On
steps.instructions: If the MRx powers on far enough to allow testing, perform an Operational Check according to facility procedure and document whether it passes or reports a specific failure.

steps.title: Review Available Status Information
steps.instructions: If accessible, review the Status Log in Service Mode or through the approved service process. Document repeated failure messages, timestamps, test failures, or readiness status changes.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Was External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears after correcting power, battery seating, or a faulty accessory, repeat startup and readiness checks. Verify the Ready For Use indicator returns to normal.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears, the Operational Check passes, and the Ready For Use indicator is normal, return the device to service according to facility policy and document final status.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displays Critical Failure Detected. Service unit. during startup. Device not considered ready for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Checked AC power, batteries, Ready For Use indicator, external condition, and accessories. Error remained present, indicating likely internal service-level failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from clinical use, labeled Out of Service, documented failure behavior, and escalated for service/bench evaluation.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Internal Memory Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for Philips HeartStart MRx Internal Memory Failure errors that may affect startup, event storage, device logs, or reliable data retention.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use if the Internal Memory Failure error appears during startup, operation, testing, or post-event review. Provide clinical staff with a ready backup defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed message is Error: Internal Memory Failure. Document when the error appears, whether it is repeatable, and whether it occurs during startup, self-test, Operational Check, or event-data review.

steps.title: Check the Ready For Use Indicator
steps.instructions: Inspect the Ready For Use indicator and document whether the device shows a normal ready status, warning condition, or service condition.

steps.title: Check Power Sources First
steps.instructions: Verify AC power, power cord seating, known-good outlet condition, and battery condition. Watch for low-voltage behavior, unexpected shutdowns, repeated rebooting, or startup instability.

steps.title: Inspect the Exterior and Access Areas
steps.instructions: Inspect the housing, card-door area, battery compartment, connectors, and exterior covers for drop damage, liquid intrusion, cracked housing, missing covers, bent contacts, contamination, or rough handling.

steps.title: Confirm the Error Is Not Related to External Data Storage
steps.instructions: If the complaint involved missing event records, failed transfer, or inability to save data, inspect approved removable media and separate external media concerns from the internal memory failure message.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check if the Device Allows It
steps.instructions: If the unit powers on safely, perform the standard Operational Check. Record whether the check passes or fails and whether the Internal Memory Failure message returns.

steps.title: Review the Status Log
steps.instructions: Review the Status Log according to facility service procedures. Look for repeated memory-related entries, startup faults, failed checks, or related service codes.

steps.title: Retest Before Any Return to Service
steps.instructions: After any approved corrective action, power-cycle the unit, confirm the error no longer appears, run required checks, verify Ready For Use status, and confirm event storage or log functions if applicable.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Error: Internal Memory Failure during startup. Staff reported concern that device memory or event-data storage may not be reliable.
CCR.Cause: Internal Memory Failure message confirmed during power-on testing. AC power, battery condition, external media, exterior condition, Ready For Use indicator, Operational Check result, and Status Log were reviewed. External causes did not clear the fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defibrillator from service and labeled Out of Service. Device sent for repair or bench evaluation due to persistent Internal Memory Failure condition.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Fan Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips HeartStart MRx Fan Failure error involving blocked ventilation, restricted airflow, debris, fan obstruction, abnormal fan operation, or internal cooling-system failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from clinical use or emergency-response staging if Fan Failure is active or recurring. Provide staff with another ready defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the device on and confirm the exact Fan Failure message. Document whether it occurs during startup, runtime, Operational Check, intermittently, or after charging, transport, cleaning, or storage.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Clinical Use
steps.instructions: Do not return the HeartStart MRx to patient care while the Fan Failure error remains active. Attach an Out of Service tag if the error is present or recurring.

steps.title: Check the Power Source and Startup Condition
steps.instructions: Connect the device to a known-good AC power outlet, confirm normal AC power indication, and test with a known-good charged battery if available.

steps.title: Inspect External Vents
steps.instructions: Inspect visible ventilation openings for blocked vents, dust, tape, labels, debris, cart obstruction, mounting obstruction, fluid residue, or contamination. Clear only external debris that can be removed safely.

steps.title: Check the Device Environment
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is not operating in an enclosed case, tightly packed crash cart drawer, transport bag, or against a surface that blocks airflow.

steps.title: Listen for Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Listen for no fan operation, brief fan operation, grinding, scraping, rattling, inconsistent operation, or normal fan sound while the error remains.

steps.title: Inspect for External Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check the housing near ventilation areas for impact damage, cracked plastic, deformation, drop damage, sticky residue, fluid intrusion, or cleaning solution buildup.

steps.title: Review Ready For Use Status
steps.instructions: Check the Ready For Use indicator. If the indicator is not normal, keep the device out of service.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check if the Device Allows It
steps.instructions: Run the available Operational Check according to facility practice. Document whether Fan Failure appears during the check or whether the check fails.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Mounting-Related Airflow Issues
steps.instructions: Remove the device from any case, shelf, mount, bracket, or transport bag that could restrict vents. Recheck operation in a clear bench setup.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If airflow restrictions were corrected, repeat startup and testing. If the fan still fails to run, makes abnormal noise, or the error remains, treat the issue as device-related.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after correcting an external airflow issue, complete an Operational Check, confirm Ready For Use status is normal, and return the device only after required checks pass.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx reported Fan Failure error during startup. Staff noted the unit was not ready for use and required Clinical Engineering evaluation.
CCR.Cause: External vents and power source were checked. No blocked vent or accessory-related obstruction was found. Fan failure condition remained, indicating likely internal fan or fan-control failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed HeartStart MRx from service, labeled Out of Service, verified failure during startup or Operational Check, and escalated for repair or fan assembly evaluation.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Power Supply Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips HeartStart MRx Power Supply Failure error affecting AC power, DC input, battery operation, charging, startup reliability, or device readiness.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use, code cart assignment, transport readiness, or emergency response staging if power reliability is in question. Provide staff with a known-good backup defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on using the reported power source if safe. Confirm the exact message Error: Power Supply Failure and document whether it appears during startup, AC operation, DC input, battery use, charging, movement, or after battery replacement.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the AC cord is fully seated at the wall outlet and device power module. Test the unit on a known-good hospital-grade outlet and verify outlet function if equipment is available.

steps.title: Inspect the AC Power Cord and External Power Module
steps.instructions: Inspect the AC cord and power module area for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, overheating, strain relief damage, looseness, contamination, or poor fit. Reseat the connection and retest.

steps.title: Check DC Input if Used
steps.instructions: If a DC input source is used, verify the DC cable, vehicle power source, or external DC supply is functional. Inspect the connector for damage, looseness, bent pins, or contamination and test with a known-good source if available.

steps.title: Check Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Power the unit off, remove the batteries, and inspect the battery compartments for poor seating, damaged contacts, corrosion, contamination, cracked housing, or latch problems. Reinstall batteries firmly and confirm proper latch engagement.

steps.title: Try Known-Good Batteries
steps.instructions: Install known-good charged HeartStart MRx batteries. Test battery-only operation, then connect AC power and verify normal charging behavior.

steps.title: Test on External Power Without Batteries
steps.instructions: If appropriate for facility process, remove the batteries and power the unit from known-good AC power. Observe whether the error appears to help separate battery-related issues from AC power path concerns.

steps.title: Test Battery Power Without External Power
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC or DC power and operate the device on known-good batteries only. Observe startup, stable operation, and whether the Power Supply Failure error returns.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Power Behavior
steps.instructions: With the device powered on and not connected to a patient, observe the unit during normal handling. Watch for display flicker, shutdown, AC icon dropout, battery icon changes, or fault return when the cord or battery area is moved.

steps.title: Review Device Indicators and Status Information
steps.instructions: Check the Ready For Use indicator and visible power or battery indicators. If available through approved service process, review status logs for repeated power supply, battery, charging, or startup events.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check if the Unit Can Safely Power On
steps.instructions: Perform the appropriate operational check according to facility procedure if the unit powers on and remains stable. Do not return the defibrillator to service if the error remains active or returns during testing.

steps.title: Isolate the Most Likely Failure Area
steps.instructions: Use results to separate failed batteries, AC cord or module problems, DC input issues, battery contact concerns, or internal power supply path failure. If the error remains on all known-good sources, remove the device from service for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Error: Power Supply Failure during startup. Staff reported unreliable power behavior and concern that AC power or battery operation could not be trusted.
CCR.Cause: Known-good outlet, AC power connection, DC input, battery seating, battery compartment contacts, and known-good batteries were checked. Error persisted on multiple power sources, indicating likely internal power supply path or power-related assembly failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed HeartStart MRx from clinical service, labeled device Out of Service, documented power source and battery checks, and escalated for bench repair or service evaluation.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Shock Equip Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips HeartStart MRx Shock Equip Malfunction error affecting defibrillation readiness, shock delivery reliability, therapy accessories, or internal therapy hardware.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use if it is being relied on for defibrillation, cardioversion, pacing, or emergency response. Provide clinical staff with another verified defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and confirm that Shock Equip Malfunction is displayed or recorded. Document whether it appears during startup, Operational Check, accessory connection, charging, or intermittent use.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Defib Therapy Use Until Verified
steps.instructions: Do not return the MRx to clinical use for defibrillation or synchronized cardioversion based only on temporarily clearing the message. Require appropriate operational verification before return to service.

steps.title: Check the Power Source and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Connect AC power, verify normal power indicators, inspect battery seating and contacts, and test with a known-good charged battery if available.

steps.title: Inspect the Therapy Cable, Pads, or Paddles
steps.instructions: Inspect therapy accessories for cuts, crushed areas, bent pins, missing insulation, damaged strain relief, contamination, loose connectors, expired pads, or damaged paddle contacts.

steps.title: Reseat the Therapy Cable or Paddles
steps.instructions: Power the unit off, disconnect and reconnect the therapy cable, pads adapter, or paddles, confirm a secure therapy port connection, then power the unit back on and observe whether the error returns.

steps.title: Inspect the Therapy Port
steps.instructions: Examine the therapy connector port for bent pins, broken plastic, fluid residue, corrosion, debris, or impact damage. Do not force damaged connectors into the port.

steps.title: Test With Known-Good Therapy Accessories
steps.instructions: Swap in known-good therapy cable, pads adapter, or paddles if available. Repeat startup and run the Operational Check to determine whether the original accessory is defective.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check
steps.instructions: Perform the HeartStart MRx Operational Check according to facility procedure. Document whether the shock equipment malfunction appears during the test or whether any therapy-related test fails.

steps.title: Review Available Status or Failure Information
steps.instructions: Check device status indicators and any available service or status log information per department procedure. Look for repeated shock equipment errors, failed Operational Checks, or therapy-related failures.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears with known-good accessories and the MRx passes Operational Check, replace the defective accessory. If the error remains with known-good accessories, remove the device from service for internal therapy system evaluation.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after correcting an accessory, cable, port contamination, or connection issue, run the required Operational Check and return the defibrillator to service only after verification passes.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Shock Equip Malfunction. Staff reported the unit was not considered reliable for defibrillation therapy.
CCR.Cause: Therapy cable, pads/paddles, therapy port, battery, and power source were inspected. Error persisted during Operational Check with known-good therapy accessories, indicating likely internal therapy hardware failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed HeartStart MRx from service, labeled unit Out of Service, and escalated for bench evaluation/repair of therapy circuitry or related therapy assembly. Device not returned to service.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Pacer Equip Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting Error: Pacer Equip Malfunction on the Philips HeartStart MRx when external pacing may be unreliable or unavailable due to pads, therapy cable, power, connector, or internal therapy circuitry issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify clinical staff immediately, do not rely on the device for external pacing while the error is active, and provide an alternate defibrillator/monitor with pacing capability if pacing may be required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and confirm the exact message shown is Error: Pacer Equip Malfunction. Observe whether it appears during startup, pacer mode, cable or pad connection, Operational Check, or movement.

steps.title: Remove the Device from Pacing Use
steps.instructions: Label the device as not acceptable for pacing use until corrected. If facility policy requires full removal from service for therapy-related errors, remove the MRx from clinical use entirely.

steps.title: Inspect the Multifunction Electrode Pads
steps.instructions: Check for expired pads, damaged packaging, dried gel, incorrect pad type, contaminated connectors, or pads that were previously opened or partially used. Replace with known-good compatible multifunction electrode pads.

steps.title: Check the Therapy Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect and reseat the therapy cable. Look for bent pins, cracked connector housing, loose locking tabs, cable cuts, strain damage, fluid contamination, or evidence of repeated pulling or twisting.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Therapy Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the installed therapy cable with a known-good compatible cable. Reconnect known-good pads or a proper test load, power cycle the device, and check whether the error returns.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the MRx exterior and therapy connector area for cracked housing, loose connector, bent or recessed contacts, fluid intrusion, corrosion, burn marks, or signs of impact damage.

steps.title: Verify Power Condition
steps.instructions: Connect to known-good AC power, confirm the battery is installed and seated correctly, and try a known-good charged battery if available. Observe whether the error appears on AC, battery, or both.

steps.title: Run the Operational Check
steps.instructions: Perform the Philips HeartStart MRx Operational Check according to facility procedure and document whether the pacer-related portion passes or fails.

steps.title: Perform Pacer-Related Bench Testing
steps.instructions: Use approved clinical engineering test equipment to verify whether the device can enter pacer mode and whether pacing output is detected correctly through the therapy cable. Do not test pacing on a patient.

steps.title: Review Status Logs or Service Records
steps.instructions: Review the device status log, error history, or recent service history for repeated therapy, pacer, cable, power, or self-test failures.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears after replacing pads or the therapy cable, replace the failed accessory and complete testing. If the error remains with known-good accessories and stable power, treat the fault as internal.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears and the MRx passes Operational Check and pacer-related testing, return the device to service and document the accessory replaced, test results, and final status.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Error: Pacer Equip Malfunction. Staff reported the unit could not be trusted for external pacing.
CCR.Cause: Known-good multifunction pads and therapy cable were tested. Error remained during Operational Check, indicating likely internal therapy/pacing circuitry failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from clinical use, labeled Out of Service, documented failed pacer-related testing, and sent unit for repair/bench evaluation.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: ECG Equip Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting ECG Equip Malfunction on the Philips HeartStart MRx when ECG monitoring hardware, electrodes, leadwires, patient cable, port, configuration, or internal ECG circuitry may be unreliable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Transfer monitoring to a reliable backup monitor or defibrillator before troubleshooting. Do not rely on the HeartStart MRx for ECG monitoring while the ECG Equip Malfunction error is active or unresolved.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed message is Error: ECG Equip Malfunction. Note whether it occurs on startup, during monitoring, after cable connection, intermittently with cable movement, or continuously.

steps.title: Check ECG Electrodes
steps.instructions: Inspect for expired electrodes, dried gel, poor skin contact, loose placement, moisture, lotion, hair, or incorrect placement. Replace with new, in-date electrodes if appropriate.

steps.title: Check Leadwires and ECG Patient Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect leadwires and cable for bent snaps, broken clips, frayed insulation, stretched wires, cuts, kinks, crushed sections, fluid intrusion, connector damage, or intermittent behavior when moved.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good ECG Cable
steps.instructions: Install a known-good compatible ECG cable and test with electrodes or an ECG simulator. Restart the device if needed to see whether the error clears.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Port
steps.instructions: Check the HeartStart MRx ECG input port for bent pins, debris, corrosion, broken housing, loose fit, fluid contamination, or damage from forced cable insertion.

steps.title: Test With an ECG Simulator
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good ECG cable to an ECG simulator and verify a stable ECG waveform across available leads without the equipment malfunction message.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check
steps.instructions: Run the HeartStart MRx Operational Check according to facility procedure and review results for ECG-related failures or repeated malfunction messages.

steps.title: Escalate if External Causes Are Ruled Out
steps.instructions: If the error remains with known-good electrodes, leadwires, ECG cable, ECG port inspection, correct configuration, and simulator testing, remove the device from service and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx reported Error: ECG Equip Malfunction. Staff reported ECG monitoring was unavailable or intermittent and the device could not be trusted for patient rhythm monitoring.
CCR.Cause: ECG cable, leadwires, electrodes, ECG port, and setup were evaluated. Known-good ECG cable and simulator testing did not clear the malfunction, indicating likely internal ECG monitoring hardware or related PCA connection failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from clinical use and labeled Out of Service. Referred unit for bench evaluation and repair of ECG monitoring circuitry and related internal connections. Device not returned to service pending repair and verification.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: ECG Cable Failure
description: Troubleshooting ECG Cable Failure errors causing ECG lead monitoring loss, poor ECG signal, intermittent waveform dropout, or failed cable detection on the Philips HeartStart MRx.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Transfer monitoring to another device before troubleshooting if ECG monitoring is unreliable. Remove the unit from active patient use if the ECG cable error affects confidence in monitoring, pacing, defibrillation readiness, or emergency response.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message is Error: ECG Cable Failure. Observe whether it appears at startup, when the ECG cable is connected, when leads are attached, during movement, or during Operational Check.

steps.title: Check the ECG Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the ECG cable at the MRx ECG port. Confirm the connector is fully seated, aligned correctly, and not loose or strained.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Cable for Obvious Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the cable, connector, strain relief, leadwire junctions, and lead ends for bent pins, cracked housing, damaged insulation, exposed conductors, broken wires, contamination, corrosion, or loose snap and clip connections.

steps.title: Check for Fluid or Cleaning-Related Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the cable ends and device connector area for moisture, residue, dried cleaning solution, corrosion, or debris. Remove the device from use if contamination may affect reliable ECG detection.

steps.title: Verify Leadwire and Electrode Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct ECG lead set is being used. Check electrodes, snaps, clips, lead labels, simulator connections, and signal output to rule out setup-related complaints.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good ECG Cable
steps.instructions: Install a known-good compatible ECG cable and test ECG operation with a simulator when available. If the error clears and waveform is stable, replace the failed cable.

steps.title: Flex-Test the Suspect Cable
steps.instructions: For intermittent issues, gently flex the cable near the device connector, strain relief points, leadwire junctions, and lead ends while watching for artifact, dropout, or return of the error.

steps.title: Inspect the MRx ECG Port
steps.instructions: If known-good cables also fail, inspect the MRx ECG port for bent or recessed pins, cracked housing, loose connector fit, foreign material, fluid contamination, or impact damage.

steps.title: Run an Operational Check
steps.instructions: Run the HeartStart MRx Operational Check according to facility procedure using a known-good ECG cable and simulator. Confirm the ECG function passes before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Isolate the Failure
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue follows the ECG cable, a lead set, an accessory, contamination, the ECG port, or the device-side ECG acquisition path.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If replacing the ECG cable clears the error and ECG verification or Operational Check passes, document the repair and return the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed ECG Cable Failure error and would not provide reliable ECG monitoring. Staff reported intermittent ECG dropout when the cable was connected.
CCR.Cause: ECG cable failed inspection and reproduced the error when flexed near the connector. Known-good ECG cable cleared the error and produced stable ECG waveform on simulator.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced failed ECG cable. Verified proper ECG waveform with simulator and confirmed no ECG Cable Failure error present. Operational Check completed and device returned to service.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Pads Cable Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for Philips HeartStart MRx Pads Cable Failure when the defibrillator does not properly detect the multifunction pads cable used for therapy functions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use if it is connected to a patient or may be needed for defibrillation, pacing, or synchronized cardioversion. Provide a verified backup defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed message is Pads Cable Failure. Note whether it appears at startup, when the pads cable is connected, when pads are attached, during Operational Check, or intermittently when the cable is moved.

steps.title: Inspect the Multifunction Pads Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the pads cable for cracked insulation, kinks, crushed sections, exposed conductors, damaged strain relief, loose connector housing, fluid contamination, residue, or damaged connector pins.

steps.title: Check Cable Seating at the Therapy Connector
steps.instructions: Reconnect the pads cable firmly to the MRx therapy port. Confirm it is fully seated and observe whether the error appears, clears, or changes when the connector or cable is gently moved.

steps.title: Inspect the MRx Therapy Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the therapy port for bent pins, debris, corrosion, fluid residue, damaged guide features, loose contacts, recessed contacts, or impact damage. Do not force the connector into a damaged port.

steps.title: Try Known-Good Pads
steps.instructions: Attach new or known-good compatible multifunction pads. Verify pads are not expired, damaged, opened, dried out, contaminated, or incompatible.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Pads Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the suspect pads cable with a known-good compatible pads cable. Power cycle the MRx if needed and verify whether the error returns.

steps.title: Perform an Operational Check
steps.instructions: Run the MRx Operational Check according to facility procedure. Confirm therapy cable and pads-related checks pass and verify normal readiness indicators before return to service.

steps.title: Isolate the Failure
steps.instructions: Use swap results to determine whether the issue follows the pads cable, pads, connector movement, therapy port, or remains with the device using known-good accessories.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after replacing the pads cable or pads, complete an Operational Check and verify normal readiness indicators before returning the MRx to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Pads Cable Failure and staff reported the device did not properly detect the multifunction pads cable.
CCR.Cause: Multifunction pads cable was found damaged at the strain relief and failed detection. Device passed testing with known-good pads cable and pads.
CCR.Resolution: Removed failed pads cable from service, installed known-good compatible pads cable, performed Operational Check, and verified no Pads Cable Failure returned.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Paddles Cable Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Philips HeartStart MRx Paddles Cable Failure error when external paddles are not detected correctly, affecting manual defibrillation readiness and paddle-based therapy reliability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Philips HeartStart MRx from patient use if the error appears during setup, testing, or clinical readiness checks. Do not rely on external paddles for patient therapy while this error is unresolved. Provide a backup defibrillator or alternate therapy method according to facility protocol.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the MRx on and confirm the exact error displayed is Paddles Cable Failure. Observe whether it occurs at startup, when paddles are connected, when paddles are reseated, during operational check, or during paddle test setup.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Clinical Readiness Until Checked
steps.instructions: If the MRx is assigned to a crash cart, treatment area, or transport role, notify clinical staff that the unit should not remain in service until the paddles issue is corrected.

steps.title: Inspect the External Paddles
steps.instructions: Examine both paddles for cracked housings, broken controls, stuck buttons, contamination, fluid intrusion, and damaged contact surfaces.

steps.title: Inspect the Paddles Cable and Strain Relief
steps.instructions: Check the full cable for cuts, kinks, pinching, crushed areas, exposed conductors, stretched sections, or intermittent behavior when the cable is gently flexed.

steps.title: Inspect the Therapy Connector
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the paddles cable at the MRx therapy connector. Inspect for bent pins, recessed pins, damaged sockets, debris, fluid residue, poor seating, or loose connector fit.

steps.title: Check the Paddle Tray and Test Load Path
steps.instructions: Place the paddles correctly in the tray or test position. Inspect the tray and test load contact area for damage, contamination, or obstruction, then run the appropriate operational check.

steps.title: Clean External Contact Areas if Needed
steps.instructions: Clean contaminated paddle contacts, tray contacts, or connector exterior using facility-approved procedures and manufacturer-compatible cleaning practices. Allow areas to dry fully before retesting.

steps.title: Try Known-Good External Paddles if Available
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible external paddles set to the MRx, power cycle the device, and repeat the operational check to determine whether the error follows the original paddles assembly.

steps.title: Check for Device-Side Connector Damage
steps.instructions: If known-good paddles also fail on the original MRx, inspect the device-side therapy connector area for mechanical damage, looseness, broken latch points, or signs of impact. Do not perform internal disassembly unless authorized and trained.

steps.title: Run Required Operational and Safety Checks After Any Correction
steps.instructions: After reseating, cleaning, or replacing paddles, perform required MRx operational and readiness checks before returning the defibrillator to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Paddles Cable Failure during readiness check. External paddles were not detected properly, preventing reliable paddle-based therapy readiness.
CCR.Cause: External paddles cable connection issue found at the therapy connector. Cable was reseated and connector area inspected. No device-side damage observed.
CCR.Resolution: Inspected paddles, cable strain relief, connector pins, and paddle tray/test load path. Reseated paddles cable and performed operational check. Error cleared and device passed testing. Returned to service.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Pads ECG Equip Failure
description: Troubleshooting Pads ECG Equip Failure on the Philips HeartStart MRx when pads ECG monitoring is unavailable or unreliable through the therapy pads signal path.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on pads ECG monitoring from the HeartStart MRx if the pads ECG signal is unreliable. Transfer monitoring to another ECG source or replace the defibrillator if patient care requires active monitoring or therapy readiness.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message displayed is Pads ECG Equip Failure. Observe whether it occurs at startup, when pads are connected, during monitoring, when switching to pads ECG view, or intermittently with movement of the cable or connector.

steps.title: Remove and Reconnect the Multifunction Pads
steps.instructions: Disconnect and inspect the multifunction pads connector for bent pins, contamination, fluid, gel residue, torn packaging, expired pads, or incomplete seating. Reconnect the pads firmly.

steps.title: Try Known-Good Multifunction Pads
steps.instructions: Replace the installed pads with known-good, compatible Philips multifunction pads. Do not reuse questionable, dried, expired, contaminated, or damaged pads.

steps.title: Inspect the Pads Cable
steps.instructions: Check the pads cable for cuts, crushed insulation, strain relief damage, bent or loose connectors, fluid intrusion, intermittent signal when flexed, or cleaning chemical damage.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Pads Cable
steps.instructions: Swap the pads cable with a known-good compatible cable, reconnect known-good pads, and check whether the error clears.

steps.title: Inspect the Therapy Connector / Therapy Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the therapy connector on the MRx for bent contacts, broken plastic, loose connector fit, fluid residue, debris, corrosion, or damage from cable strain or impact.

steps.title: Check for Contamination or Cleaning Residue
steps.instructions: Inspect the therapy port and pads cable connector for dried cleaner, gel, fluids, or debris. Clean only according to facility-approved and manufacturer-safe cleaning practices and allow the area to dry before retesting.

steps.title: Compare Pads ECG to Lead ECG Function
steps.instructions: Verify whether standard ECG monitoring works through the ECG lead input, then test pads ECG monitoring using known-good pads, cable, or simulator connection as appropriate for your shop process.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Retest
steps.instructions: Turn the MRx off, disconnect questionable accessories, reconnect known-good pads and cable, then restart the device and confirm whether the error returns.

steps.title: Perform Functional Verification
steps.instructions: Verify that the Pads ECG Equip Failure message does not return, the pads cable connection is stable, pads ECG signal is present when selected, the therapy connector remains secure, and the device passes required operational checks.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed Pads ECG Equip Failure. Staff reported pads ECG monitoring was unavailable through multifunction pads.
CCR.Cause: Known-good pads and pads cable corrected the issue. Original pads cable caused intermittent pads ECG signal failure when moved.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defective pads cable from service, installed known-good compatible pads cable, verified pads ECG monitoring operation, confirmed error did not return, and returned device to service.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: NBP Equip Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting guide for NBP Equip Malfunction on the Philips HeartStart MRx when non-invasive blood pressure measurement is unreliable, aborted, or unavailable.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If NBP monitoring is needed, transfer blood pressure monitoring to another approved device and do not rely on the MRx NBP function while the error is active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error: NBP Equip Malfunction and observe whether it occurs at startup, NBP selection, cuff inflation, measurement, or intermittently after movement.

steps.title: Check the NBP Cuff
steps.instructions: Inspect the cuff for tears, worn closure material, bladder damage, contamination, or incorrect sizing. Test with a known-good compatible cuff.

steps.title: Check the NBP Hose and Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect the hose for cracks, kinks, restrictions, loose fittings, or contamination. Replace with a known-good compatible hose if available.

steps.title: Inspect the NBP Connector at the MRx
steps.instructions: Check the NBP port for damage, looseness, debris, cracked plastic, bent fittings, or poor hose engagement. Reconnect the hose firmly.

steps.title: Check for External Leak Behavior
steps.instructions: Run an NBP cycle with known-good accessories and listen for leaks while watching for weak inflation, rapid deflation, or repeated failed attempts.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Fluid Exposure
steps.instructions: Inspect the housing and NBP connector area for impact damage, residue, cleaning-fluid intrusion, or signs the unit was dropped.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Is Not Accessory-Related
steps.instructions: Repeat testing with a known-good cuff and hose. If the error clears, remove the failed accessory from service and verify normal NBP operation.

steps.title: Evaluate for Internal Tubing or Module Failure
steps.instructions: If the error remains with known-good accessories, suspect internal NBP pneumatic or hardware failure and remove the unit from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If accessory replacement resolves the issue, document the failed accessory, complete an NBP functional check, clean the unit as needed, and return it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx reported Error: NBP Equip Malfunction. Staff reported NBP would not complete measurements and cuff inflation failed or aborted during use.
CCR.Cause: Checked NBP cuff, hose, connector, and external pneumatic path. Known-good cuff and hose did not clear the error. Findings indicate likely internal NBP hardware malfunction.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defibrillator from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench evaluation and repair. NBP functional checks and calibration required before return to service.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: SpO2 Equip Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting guide for SpO2 Equip Malfunction on the Philips HeartStart MRx when SpO2 monitoring is unavailable, unreliable, or affected by accessory or device-side hardware faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Philips HeartStart MRx from patient use if SpO2 monitoring is needed and the device is displaying SpO2 Equip Malfunction. Use an alternate monitor, defibrillator, or pulse oximeter before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed message is SpO2 Equip Malfunction. Note whether it appears at startup, after connecting the sensor, during monitoring, intermittently with movement, or continuously.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Sensor First
steps.instructions: Inspect the sensor for cracks, contamination, damaged emitter or detector area, bent pins, fluid intrusion, strain, or cuts. Test with a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Patient Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 extension or patient cable for bent pins, loose fit, crushed sections, cuts, strain, contamination, or intermittent readings when moved. Replace with a known-good compatible cable.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Connector Area on the MRx
steps.instructions: Inspect the MRx SpO2 connector for bent or recessed pins, debris, fluid residue, loose housing, cracked panel, or impact damage. Confirm the known-good cable seats securely.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Retest
steps.instructions: Power the MRx off, disconnect the SpO2 cable and sensor, reconnect known-good accessories, power the device back on, and verify whether SpO2 monitoring returns without the error.

steps.title: Isolate Whether the Issue Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Use known-good sensors and cables, test suspected accessories on another compatible monitor if available, and determine whether the fault follows the accessory or remains with the MRx.

steps.title: Perform Final External Functional Verification
steps.instructions: Verify no SpO2 Equip Malfunction message, proper SpO2 value display, present pleth waveform, stable cable connection, expected SpO2 alarms, and required operational checks before return to service.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx displayed SpO2 Equip Malfunction and would not provide reliable SpO2 monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Known-good SpO2 sensor and cable were tested. Error remained with the MRx after connector inspection and power cycle, indicating likely device-side SpO2 hardware failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service, labeled Out of Service, documented external checks performed, and sent unit for bench evaluation or repair.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: CO2 Equip Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting CO2 Equip Malfunction on a Philips HeartStart MRx defibrillator when EtCO2 monitoring is unavailable, unreliable, or unable to initialize due to FilterLine, sampling path, connection, or CO2 hardware concerns.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on CO2 monitoring while CO2 Equip Malfunction is active. Provide alternate EtCO2 monitoring if clinically required and confirm patient care is supported by a reliable device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact error is CO2 Equip Malfunction. Note whether it appears at startup, when CO2 is selected, after connecting a FilterLine, during monitoring, or intermittently during movement or transport.

steps.title: Check the FilterLine First
steps.instructions: Inspect the FilterLine or sampling line for kinks, crushed tubing, moisture, clogging, contamination, incorrect connection, or expired accessory use. Replace it with a known-good compatible FilterLine.

steps.title: Inspect the CO2 Inlet and Sampling Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the CO2 inlet area for debris, fluid, contamination, connector damage, or poor accessory seating. Confirm the FilterLine seats fully and securely.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Fluid Exposure
steps.instructions: Check for cracked housing, impact damage, fluid intrusion, cleaning residue, corrosion, or contamination near the CO2 connector area.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Retest
steps.instructions: Power the MRx off, disconnect the FilterLine, wait briefly, restart the device, reconnect a known-good compatible FilterLine, and confirm whether CO2 initializes normally.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Is Specific to CO2
steps.instructions: Verify ECG, SpO2 if available, NBP if equipped, therapy controls, battery operation, AC operation, and startup behavior. Multiple failures may indicate a broader device issue.

steps.title: Review Logs or Operational Check Results
steps.instructions: Run the appropriate operational check if permitted and review available status logs for CO2-related self-test, initialization, or communication failures.

steps.title: Retest With Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Use approved known-good CO2 sampling accessories and confirm the accessory type is compatible with the device configuration.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after external correction, complete a final operational check, verify CO2 waveform and numeric value when a valid sample is present, and document the final device status.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx reported CO2 Equip Malfunction. Staff stated CO2 would not initialize and no EtCO2 waveform or numeric value was available during monitoring setup.
CCR.Cause: CO2 error was confirmed during startup and CO2 selection. Known-good compatible FilterLine was tested, CO2 inlet was inspected for blockage or contamination, and the fault remained after external checks.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service and labeled Out of Service for bench evaluation. External CO2 accessory and sampling path checks were completed, and persistent CO2 Equip Malfunction was documented for repair.

title: Philips HeartStart MRx Defibrillator - Error: Printer Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting Printer Malfunction errors on the Philips HeartStart MRx that may cause failed strip printing, printer self-test failure, blank or incomplete printouts, paper feed issues, or printer communication failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the HeartStart MRx from patient use if printing is required for patient care, code documentation, rhythm review, or event recording. If the device is actively needed for defibrillation, monitoring, pacing, or critical use, provide a backup defibrillator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed message is Error: Printer Malfunction. Check whether the error appears during startup, operational check, print attempt, event summary printing, or intermittent use. Confirm associated behavior such as no printing, no paper feed, blank printout, incomplete printout, failed self-test, printer door issue, or printer communication failure.

steps.title: Check Printer Paper First
steps.instructions: Open the printer door and verify that approved thermal paper is installed correctly. Confirm the roll is not empty, jammed, backwards, wrinkled, wet, contaminated, or binding inside the printer compartment. Reload the paper and attempt a print test.

steps.title: Check the Printer Door and Latch
steps.instructions: Inspect the printer door for proper closure. Verify the latch is not cracked, loose, obstructed, or prevented from fully closing by paper, debris, or damage. Open and close the printer door firmly, then retry printing.

steps.title: Inspect the Printer Mechanism Externally
steps.instructions: With the device removed from active use, inspect the visible printer area for torn paper, paper dust buildup, label adhesive, contamination, obstructed paper path, damaged roller, loose parts, impact damage, or fluid intrusion. Do not perform deep disassembly at the clinical area.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Turn the HeartStart MRx off, wait briefly, then power it back on using a known-good battery or AC power source. Observe whether the Printer Malfunction error returns during startup, operational check, or print attempt.

steps.title: Run or Review the Printer Self-Test / Operational Check
steps.instructions: Perform the appropriate operational check or printer-related test available through the device workflow. Confirm whether the printer passes or fails. Verify that paper feeds correctly and the printout is legible.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Configuration Confusion
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are not confusing printer failure with other documentation or workflow issues. Check whether event summary printing was selected properly, paper was loaded correctly, and thermal paper orientation is correct.

steps.title: Isolate Printer Assembly vs Communication Path
steps.instructions: If the printer fails self-test consistently, suspect the printer assembly first. If the printer appears mechanically functional but the device continues to report Printer Malfunction or communication failure, remove the device from service for bench evaluation, including printer data cable and communication path checks.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: After correcting paper loading, door closure, obstruction, or an accessible connection during bench evaluation, perform a complete operational check. Confirm no Printer Malfunction error returns, printer feeds correctly, printout is legible, and the device is safe for clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Philips HeartStart MRx reported Error: Printer Malfunction. Staff stated the printer would not print rhythm strips and the error appeared during operational check.
CCR.Cause: Printer paper and door were checked and reloaded. No paper jam or external obstruction found. Printer self-test continued to fail, indicating likely printer assembly failure.
